snippets: (loop for i from 1 to 5 collect (* i i))

files.content:
;;;; hello.lisp — load me with (load "hello.lisp") at the REPL

(defun greet (name)
  "Return a friendly greeting for NAME."
  (format nil "Hello, ~a!" name))

(defun factorial (n)
  "Classic recursion — the shape of Lisp."
  (if (<= n 1)
      1
      (* n (factorial (- n 1)))))

(format t "~a~%" (greet "Lisp"))
(format t "10! = ~a~%" (factorial 10))

spaces.tutorial:
# Common Lisp

Welcome to the language that has been quietly ahead of everyone since 1958. In Lisp, code *is* data: every program is a list you can build, inspect, and transform with the same tools you use on any other list. The REPL isn't a side feature — it's where you live.

## Your REPL

Press **START** on the REPL device. When you see the `[1]>` prompt, `clisp` is listening. Click **HINTS** for starter expressions, or type these yourself:

```lisp
(+ 1 2 3 4 5)              ; prefix notation: the operator comes first
(defun square (x) (* x x)) ; define a function
(square 12)                ; call it
(mapcar #'square '(1 2 3 4 5)) ; map it over a list
```

Every form returns a value the REPL prints. Parentheses aren't clutter — they're the structure.

## The sample

`hello.lisp` defines two functions. Load it into your running REPL with:

```lisp
(load "hello.lisp")
```

- `greet` uses `format nil` to build a string (nil = return it rather than print).
- `factorial` is the textbook recursion: a base case and a self-call.

After loading, call them live: `(greet "world")`, `(factorial 20)`.

## Try this

1. Define `cube` the way `square` is defined, and map it over `'(1 2 3)`.
2. Use `(loop for i from 1 to 10 collect i)` to build a list, then `reduce` it with `#'+`.
3. Ask the REPL what a symbol is: type `'hello` (quoted) versus `hello` (evaluated — it'll error, which teaches you what evaluation means).

## Going further

The free, complete classic: [Practical Common Lisp](https://gigamonkeys.com/book/). To quit the REPL, type `(quit)` or press STOP.

spaces.name: Python
spaces.blurb: The friendly all-rounder: clean syntax you read like English, batteries included for almost anything.
spaces.tutorial:
# Python

Python is the friendly all-rounder of programming languages. Its syntax reads almost like English, whitespace organizes your code instead of curly braces, and a huge standard library means "batteries are included" for files, web, math, and more. It's a superb first language and a lifelong tool.

## Your REPL

Click **START** on the REPL device to launch `python3`. You'll see the `>>>` prompt. Try these one at a time:

```python
print('hello, world')
sum(range(1, 101))
[x * x for x in range(10)]
def greet(name): return f'Hello, {name}!'
greet('Python')
sorted('the quick brown fox'.split(), key=len)
```

The REPL prints the value of each expression immediately, so it's perfect for experimenting.

## The sample

Open `hello.py`:

```python
def greet(name):
    return f'Hello, {name}!'
```

`def` defines a function. The `f'...'` string is an **f-string**: `{name}` is replaced with the argument's value.

```python
def squares(n):
    return [x * x for x in range(n)]
```

This returns a **list comprehension** — a compact way to build a list. `range(n)` yields `0..n-1`.

```python
if __name__ == '__main__':
    print(greet('world'))
    print('first five squares:', squares(5))
```

This block runs only when the file is executed directly (not imported). It's the conventional Python entry point.

## Try this

1. Change `greet('world')` to greet yourself, then run the snippet in the REPL.
2. Add a `cubes(n)` function that returns cubes instead of squares.
3. In the REPL, try `{w: len(w) for w in 'red green blue'.split()}` to build a dict comprehension.

## Going further

Official tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/

spaces.name: JavaScript (Node)
spaces.blurb: The language of the web, now everywhere — run it in the browser, on servers, and on the command line.
spaces.tutorial:
# JavaScript (Node)

JavaScript started as the language of web browsers and now runs everywhere thanks to **Node.js**, which lets you execute JS on servers and the command line. It's dynamic, event-driven, and has the largest package ecosystem on Earth (npm).

## Your REPL

Click **START** on the REPL device to launch `node`. You'll see the `>` prompt. Try these in order:

```javascript
console.log('hello, world')
[1, 2, 3, 4].map(n => n * n)
[5, 1, 4, 2, 3].sort((a, b) => a - b)
const greet = name => `Hello, ${name}!`
greet('Node')
'the quick brown fox'.split(' ').filter(w => w.length > 3)
```

The REPL echoes each expression's result. `undefined` after `console.log` is normal — it's the return value of the log call.

## The sample

Open `hello.mjs` (the `.mjs` extension enables ES modules):

```javascript
function greet(name) {
  return `Hello, ${name}!`;
}
```

Backticks make a **template literal**; `${name}` interpolates the value.

```javascript
function squares(n) {
  return Array.from({ length: n }, (_, i) => i * i);
}
```

`Array.from` builds an array from a length and a mapping function. `(_, i)` ignores the first arg and uses the index `i`.

```javascript
console.log(greet('world'));
console.log('first five squares:', squares(5));
```

Run the file from a terminal with `node hello.mjs`.

## Try this

1. Add a `cubes(n)` function using `i * i * i`.
2. In the REPL, try `[1,2,3,4,5].reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0)` to sum an array.
3. Explore `Object.entries({a: 1, b: 2})` and map over the pairs.

## Going further

MDN JavaScript guide: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Guide

spaces.tutorial:
# TypeScript

TypeScript is JavaScript with **static types**. You annotate variables and functions, and the compiler catches whole categories of bugs — typos, wrong argument types, missing fields — before your code ever runs. It compiles down to plain JavaScript, so it runs anywhere JS does.

## Your REPL

Click **START** on the REPL device to launch `npx ts-node` (the first run downloads it; give it a moment). At the `>` prompt, try these in order:

```typescript
const greet = (name: string): string => `Hello, ${name}!`
greet('TypeScript')
const nums: number[] = [1, 2, 3, 4]
nums.map(n => n * n)
type Point = { x: number; y: number }
const p: Point = { x: 3, y: 4 }; Math.hypot(p.x, p.y)
```

Try giving `greet` a number instead of a string — the REPL will complain, which is the whole point.

## The sample

Open `hello.ts`:

```typescript
function greet(name: string): string {
  return `Hello, ${name}!`;
}
```

`name: string` is a **parameter type**; `: string` after the parens is the **return type**.

```typescript
type Point = { x: number; y: number };
```

A `type` alias names an object shape. Now `Point` is reusable anywhere.

```typescript
function distance(a: Point, b: Point): number {
  return Math.hypot(b.x - a.x, b.y - a.y);
}
```

The compiler guarantees `a` and `b` always have `x` and `y`. Run with `npx ts-node hello.ts`.

## Try this

1. Add a field `z: number` to `Point` and update `distance` — notice the compiler flags every call site.
2. Write a `type Shape = 'circle' | 'square'` union and a function that switches on it.
3. Make `distance` generic-free but add `readonly` to the `Point` fields.

## Going further

The TypeScript Handbook: https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/intro.html

spaces.blurb: Designed for programmer happiness: expressive, readable, and a joy to write for scripts and web alike.
spaces.tutorial:
# Ruby

Ruby was designed for **programmer happiness**. Everything is an object, blocks make iteration elegant, and the syntax stays out of your way. It powers Rails and countless scripts, and it's a delight to read aloud.

## Your REPL

Click **START** on the REPL device to launch `irb` (Interactive Ruby). At the prompt, try these in order:

```ruby
puts 'hello, world'
(1..100).sum
(0...10).map { |x| x * x }
def greet(name) = "Hello, #{name}!"
greet('Ruby')
'the quick brown fox'.split.sort_by(&:length)
```

irb prints the return value after `=>`. Note that `puts` returns `nil` — that's expected.

## The sample

Open `hello.rb`:

```ruby
def greet(name)
  "Hello, #{name}!"
end
```

Ruby methods **return the last expression** automatically — no `return` needed. `#{name}` is string interpolation.

```ruby
def squares(n)
  (0...n).map { |x| x * x }
end
```

`(0...n)` is a range excluding `n`. `.map { |x| ... }` transforms each element; `|x|` names the block parameter.

```ruby
puts greet('world')
puts "first five squares: #{squares(5).inspect}"
```

`.inspect` gives a readable representation of the array. Run with `ruby hello.rb`.

## Try this

1. Rewrite `squares` using `.select` to keep only even squares.
2. In irb, try `%w[red green blue].each_with_index.to_h` to pair words with indices.
3. Add a `cubes` method and print both from the sample.

## Going further

Ruby documentation: https://www.ruby-lang.org/en/documentation/

spaces.blurb: Simple, fast, and built for concurrency — one binary out, no fuss, compiles in the blink of an eye.
spaces.tutorial:
# Go

Go (golang) is a small, fast, statically typed language from Google. It compiles almost instantly to a single self-contained binary, has garbage collection, and makes concurrency easy with goroutines. There's no REPL in the standard toolchain — you learn Go by editing and running.

## Your REPL

Go has no built-in REPL, so this space uses **run** mode. Click **RUN** to compile and run `hello.go` with `go run hello.go`. Edit the file, click RUN again, and see your changes immediately.

## The sample

Open `hello.go`:

```go
package main

import "fmt"
```

Every Go program lives in a package; `main` is the entry package. `fmt` is the formatting/printing library.

```go
func greet(name string) string {
	return fmt.Sprintf("Hello, %s!", name)
}
```

Types come **after** the name (`name string`). `Sprintf` formats a string; `%s` is a string placeholder.

```go
func squares(n int) []int {
	out := make([]int, n)
	for i := 0; i < n; i++ {
		out[i] = i * i
	}
	return out
}
```

`[]int` is a slice of ints. `:=` declares and infers a type. The classic `for` loop fills it.

```go
func main() {
	fmt.Println(greet("world"))
	fmt.Println("first five squares:", squares(5))
}
```

`main` runs when you execute the program.

## Try this

1. Add a `cubes(n int) []int` function and print it from `main`.
2. Change the `for` loop to use `range`: `for i := range out { out[i] = i * i }`.
3. Add a second import and print the current time with `time.Now()`.

## Going further

A Tour of Go: https://go.dev/tour/

spaces.name: Rust
spaces.blurb: Memory safety without a garbage collector — fearless concurrency and C-level speed, guaranteed at compile time.
spaces.tutorial:
# Rust

Rust gives you C-level performance with a compiler that **guarantees memory safety** — no null pointers, no data races — all without a garbage collector. Its ownership system takes practice, but it catches bugs at compile time that other languages only find at runtime. There's no standard REPL, so you learn by editing and running.

## Your REPL

Rust ships no REPL in its standard toolchain, so this space uses **run** mode. Click **RUN** to compile `hello.rs` with `rustc` and execute the resulting binary. Edit, RUN, repeat.

## The sample

Open `hello.rs`:

```rust
fn greet(name: &str) -> String {
    format!("Hello, {name}!")
}
```

`&str` is a borrowed string slice; `-> String` is the return type. `format!` builds an owned `String`, interpolating `{name}` directly.

```rust
fn squares(n: u32) -> Vec<u32> {
    (0..n).map(|x| x * x).collect()
}
```

`(0..n)` is a range. `.map(|x| ...)` transforms each item; `.collect()` gathers the results into a `Vec<u32>` (a growable vector).

```rust
fn main() {
    println!("{}", greet("world"));
    println!("first five squares: {:?}", squares(5));
}
```

`{}` prints via `Display`; `{:?}` prints via `Debug` (needed for the vector).

## Try this

1. Add a `cubes(n: u32) -> Vec<u32>` function using `x * x * x`.
2. Filter to even squares: `.filter(|x| x % 2 == 0)` before `.collect()`.
3. Try making `greet` take ownership: change `&str` to `String` and see what the compiler says.

## Going further

The Rust Book: https://doc.rust-lang.org/book/

spaces.tutorial:
# Swift

Swift is Apple's modern language for iOS, macOS, watchOS, and increasingly server-side code. It's fast, memory-safe, and expressive, with a clean syntax and powerful type inference. Best of all, `swift` gives you a real interactive REPL.

## Your REPL

Click **START** on the REPL device to launch the `swift` REPL. At the `>` prompt, try these in order:

```swift
print("hello, world")
let xs = [1, 2, 3, 4]
xs.map { $0 * $0 }
print(xs.reduce(0, +))
func greet(_ name: String) -> String { "Hello, \(name)!" }
greet("Swift")
```

`$0` is the first (implicit) closure argument — a Swift shorthand you'll use constantly.

## The sample

Open `hello.swift`:

```swift
func greet(_ name: String) -> String {
    "Hello, \(name)!"
}
```

The `_` before `name` means callers omit the label. `\(name)` is string interpolation. A single-expression function returns implicitly.

```swift
func squares(_ n: Int) -> [Int] {
    (0..<n).map { $0 * $0 }
}
```

`(0..<n)` is a half-open range. `.map { ... }` transforms each element; `[Int]` is an array of ints.

```swift
print(greet("world"))
print("first five squares: \(squares(5))")
```

Run the file with `swift hello.swift`.

## Try this

1. Add a `cubes` function using `$0 * $0 * $0`.
2. Filter to even squares with `.filter { $0 % 2 == 0 }`.
3. In the REPL, try `let dict = Dictionary(uniqueKeysWithValues: xs.map { ($0, $0 * $0) })`.

## Going further

The Swift Programming Language book: https://docs.swift.org/swift-book/

spaces.name: Kotlin
spaces.blurb: A sleeker, safer take on the JVM — concise, null-safe, and the modern default for Android.
spaces.tutorial:
# Kotlin

Kotlin is a sleeker, safer language for the JVM — fully interoperable with Java but far more concise. Null safety is built into the type system, and it's the modern default for Android development. The `kotlinc` command drops you into a real REPL.

## Your REPL

Click **START** on the REPL device to launch the `kotlinc` REPL (the first launch warms up the JVM, so give it a few seconds). At the `>>>` prompt, try these in order:

```kotlin
println("hello, world")
(1..100).sum()
(0 until 10).map { it * it }
fun greet(name: String) = "Hello, $name!"
greet("Kotlin")
"the quick brown fox".split(" ").sortedBy { it.length }
```

`it` is the implicit single parameter inside a lambda — a Kotlin convenience you'll see everywhere.

## The sample

Open `hello.kt`:

```kotlin
fun greet(name: String) = "Hello, $name!"
```

An `=` function body returns the expression directly. `$name` interpolates into the string.

```kotlin
fun squares(n: Int): List<Int> = (0 until n).map { it * it }
```

`(0 until n)` is a range excluding `n`. `.map { it * it }` transforms each element; the result is a `List<Int>`.

```kotlin
fun main() {
    println(greet("world"))
    println("first five squares: ${squares(5)}")
}
```

`${...}` interpolates an expression. Run with `kotlinc hello.kt -include-runtime -d hello.jar && java -jar hello.jar`, or just `kotlin hello.kt`.

## Try this

1. Add a `cubes` function using `it * it * it`.
2. Filter to even squares: `.filter { it % 2 == 0 }`.
3. In the REPL, build a map: `(1..3).associateWith { it * it }`.

## Going further

Kotlin documentation: https://kotlinlang.org/docs/home.html

spaces.tutorial:
# PHP

PHP started as a set of "Personal Home Page" scripts in 1994 and grew into the language behind WordPress, Wikipedia, and a huge slice of the web. Modern PHP (8.x) has arrow functions, typed properties, match expressions, and a genuinely pleasant standard library. It runs anywhere and starts instantly.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch `php -a`, PHP's interactive shell. The prompt is `php >`. Every statement must end with a semicolon `;` and you use `echo` to print. Try these one at a time:

```php
echo 1 + 2;
echo strtoupper('hello');
$xs = array_map(fn($n) => $n * $n, [1,2,3,4,5]); echo implode(',', $xs);
echo array_sum(range(1, 100));
echo date('Y') . PHP_EOL;
```

Gotcha: outside the REPL, PHP code lives inside `<?php ... ?>` tags. Inside `php -a` you drop the tags and just type statements. Note there is no automatic newline after `echo` — add `PHP_EOL` or `"\n"` yourself.

## The sample

Open `hello.php`. It opens with `<?php`, greets you with string interpolation (`"Hello, {$name}!"`), then builds a list of squares with `array_map` and an arrow function `fn($n) => $n * $n`, and finally sums `range(1, 100)`.

## Try this

- Change `range(1, 5)` to `range(1, 10)` and re-run.
- Add a line that prints `strrev('desserts')`.
- Use `count()` to print how many squares you generated.

## Going further

The official manual is superb and example-rich: https://www.php.net/manual/en/

snippets: for i = 1, 5 do print(i * i) end
local s = 0; for i = 1, 100 do s = s + i end; print(s)

files.content:
print("Hello, Lua!")

local function square(n)
  return n * n
end

for i = 1, 5 do
  print(i, square(i))
end

local sum = 0
for i = 1, 100 do
  sum = sum + i
end
print("Sum 1..100 = " .. sum)

spaces.tutorial:
# Lua

Lua ("moon" in Portuguese) is small, fast, and designed to be embedded. It powers game scripting in Roblox and World of Warcraft, configuration in Neovim and Redis, and countless plugins. The whole language fits in your head: tables are the one data structure, and everything else builds on them.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch `lua`. The prompt is `>`. Print with `print(...)`, concatenate strings with `..`, and note that `1` and `1.0` are the same numeric world. Indexing starts at **1**, not 0 — a classic Lua surprise. Try:

```lua
print(1 + 2)
print(string.upper('hello'))
for i = 1, 5 do print(i * i) end
local s = 0; for i = 1, 100 do s = s + i end; print(s)
print(#'lua rocks')
```

The `#` operator gives length (of a string or table). Blocks use `do ... end`, not braces.

## The sample

Open `hello.lua`. It defines a local function `square`, loops from 1 to 5 printing each number and its square, then accumulates a running `sum` of 1..100 and prints it with `..` string concatenation.

## Try this

- Make `square` into `cube` (`n * n * n`) and re-run.
- Print `os.time()` to see the current Unix timestamp.
- Build a table `t = {10, 20, 30}` and print `#t`.

## Going further

The concise official reference manual: https://www.lua.org/manual/5.4/

files.content:

spaces.tutorial:
# Haskell

Haskell is a purely functional, statically typed language famous for its strong type system and lazy evaluation. Functions have no side effects unless their type says so (`IO`), the compiler catches whole categories of bugs before you run anything, and infinite lists are perfectly normal because nothing is computed until it is needed.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch `ghci`, the Glasgow Haskell interactive environment. The prompt is `ghci>`. You can type expressions directly and GHCi prints their value. Try these in order:

```haskell
map (^2) [1..5]
sum [1..100]
let f x = x * x
f 9
take 5 (filter even [1..])
```

Gotchas: `[1..]` is an *infinite* list — thanks to laziness, `take 5` only forces the first five. Defining a binding in GHCi uses `let` (or just `f x = x*x` on newer GHC). Ask `:t sum` to see any value's type, and `:q` to quit.

## The sample

Open `hello.hs`. `main :: IO ()` is the program entry point; the `do` block sequences three actions: print a greeting, print the squares of `[1..5]` via `map square`, and print the sum of `[1..100]`. `square` is given an explicit type signature `Int -> Int`.

## Try this

- In GHCi, evaluate `zip [1..3] "abc"`.
- Define `let double = map (*2)` then run `double [1,2,3]`.
- Try `take 10 (cycle [1,2,3])` to see laziness on an infinite loop.

## Going further

Start with the friendly official guide: https://www.haskell.org/documentation/

files.content:

squares = Enum.map(1..5, fn n -> n * n end)
IO.inspect(squares, label: "squares")

spaces.tutorial:
# Elixir

Elixir is a dynamic, functional language that runs on the Erlang VM (BEAM), inheriting decades of rock-solid concurrency and fault tolerance while adding a friendly, Ruby-flavored syntax. Its signature feature is the pipe operator `|>`, which threads data left-to-right through transformations.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch `iex`, Interactive Elixir. The prompt is `iex>`. Expressions evaluate and print immediately. Try these in order:

```elixir
1 + 2
Enum.map(1..5, & &1 * &1)
1..100 |> Enum.sum()
String.upcase("hello")
[1, 2, 3] |> Enum.reverse()
```

Gotchas: `& &1 * &1` is shorthand for an anonymous function `fn x -> x * x end` (the `&1` is the first argument). `1..5` is a Range, and `|>` feeds the value on its left as the first argument to the function on its right. Data is immutable — functions return new values.

## The sample

Open `hello.exs` (the `.exs` extension marks a script). It prints a greeting with `IO.puts`, maps `1..5` to squares and inspects them with a label, then pipes `1..100` into `Enum.sum()` and interpolates the result into a string with `#{...}`.

## Try this

- Change `Enum.map` to `Enum.filter(1..10, &(rem(&1, 2) == 0))`.
- Pipe a list through two steps: `[3,1,2] |> Enum.sort() |> Enum.reverse()`.
- Try `String.split("a,b,c", ",")`.

## Going further

The official getting-started guide: https://elixir-lang.org/getting-started/introduction.html

spaces.name: Erlang
spaces.blurb: The telecom-grade language behind nine-nines uptime, where lightweight processes chat by message.

files.content:
-module(hello).
-export([main/0]).

main() ->
    io:format("Hello, Erlang!~n"),
    Squares = lists:map(fun(N) -> N * N end, lists:seq(1, 5)),
    io:format("Squares: ~p~n", [Squares]),
    Total = lists:sum(lists:seq(1, 100)),
    io:format("Sum 1..100 = ~p~n", [Total]).

spaces.tutorial:
# Erlang

Erlang was born at Ericsson to run phone switches that must never go down. Its concurrency model — millions of cheap isolated processes that communicate only by sending messages — and its "let it crash" supervision philosophy give it legendary reliability. Elixir sits on the same VM, but Erlang's own syntax is spare and worth knowing.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch `erl`, the Erlang shell. The prompt looks like `1>`, `2>`, and so on. The single most important rule: **every expression ends with a period `.`** followed by Enter. Forget the period and the shell just waits. Try these:

```erlang
1 + 2.
lists:seq(1, 5).
lists:map(fun(N) -> N * N end, lists:seq(1, 5)).
lists:sum(lists:seq(1, 100)).
string:uppercase("hello").
```

Other gotchas: variables start with a Capital letter and are bound once (immutable). Anonymous functions are `fun(X) -> ... end`. To quit, type `q().` (with its period) or press Ctrl-C twice.

## The sample

Open `hello.erl`. It declares `-module(hello)` and exports `main/0`. Inside, `io:format` prints text (`~n` is newline, `~p` pretty-prints a term), `lists:map` squares `1..5`, and `lists:sum` totals `1..100`. Note the commas between statements and the final period.

## Try this

- In the shell: `lists:reverse([1,2,3]).`
- Bind a variable: `X = 10.` then evaluate `X * X.`
- Try `lists:filter(fun(N) -> N rem 2 =:= 0 end, lists:seq(1, 10)).`

## Going further

The official getting-started documentation: https://www.erlang.org/doc/getting_started/intro

spaces.name: Clojure
spaces.blurb: A modern Lisp on the JVM where immutable data and parentheses make concurrency almost boring.

files.content:
(println "Hello, Clojure!")

(def squares (map #(* % %) (range 1 6)))
(println "Squares:" squares)

(println "Sum 1..100 =" (reduce + (range 1 101)))

spaces.tutorial:
# Clojure

Clojure is a modern dialect of Lisp that runs on the JVM (with siblings for JavaScript and .NET). It embraces immutable, persistent data structures and a functional style, which makes concurrent code far easier to reason about. Code is data: everything is written as parenthesized lists, and the function comes first.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch `clojure`. The prompt is `user=>`. In Lisp syntax you call a function by putting it *inside* the parens: `(+ 1 2)` means "add 1 and 2". Try these in order:

```clojure
(+ 1 2)
(map #(* % %) (range 1 6))
(reduce + (range 1 101))
(clojure.string/upper-case "hello")
(reverse [1 2 3])
```

Gotchas: `#(* % %)` is shorthand for an anonymous function where `%` is the argument. `(range 1 6)` is *exclusive* of the upper bound, so it yields 1 2 3 4 5 — that is why summing 1..100 uses `(range 1 101)`. `map` is lazy; the REPL forces it when it prints.

## The sample

Open `hello.clj`. It prints a greeting with `println`, binds `squares` to the mapped squares of `(range 1 6)` using `def`, prints them, and prints the sum of `(range 1 101)` computed with `(reduce + ...)`.

## Try this

- Evaluate `(filter even? (range 1 11))`.
- Try `(count "clojure")` and `(count [1 2 3 4])`.
- Build a map: `(get {:a 1 :b 2} :b)`.

## Going further

The official learning resources and reference: https://clojure.org/guides/learn/syntax

spaces.name: Scala
spaces.blurb: Fuses object-oriented and functional programming on the JVM with a type system that means business.

files.content:
@main def run(): Unit =
  println("Hello, Scala!")

  val squares = (1 to 5).map(x => x * x)
  println(s"Squares: ${squares.mkString(", ")}")

  val total = (1 to 100).sum
  println(s"Sum 1..100 = $total")

spaces.tutorial:
# Scala

Scala blends object-oriented and functional programming into one statically typed language on the JVM. It gives you concise, expressive code with a powerful type system, seamless Java interop, and first-class support for immutable collections and pattern matching. It's the language behind Spark and much of the big-data world.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch `scala`, which opens the Scala REPL. The prompt is `scala>`. Type an expression and it shows the value and its inferred type. Try these:

```scala
1 + 2
(1 to 5).map(x => x * x)
(1 to 100).sum
"hello".toUpperCase
List(3, 1, 2).sorted
```

Gotchas: `(1 to 5)` is an inclusive `Range` (1 through 5); use `until` for exclusive. `map`, `sum`, and `sorted` are methods on collections. The REPL echoes the type (e.g. `Vector[Int]`), which is a great way to learn what you just made. Type `:quit` to exit.

## The sample

Open `hello.scala`. The `@main def run()` annotation marks the entry point (Scala 3 syntax). It prints a greeting, maps `(1 to 5)` to squares and joins them with `mkString`, then sums `(1 to 100)`. String interpolation uses the `s"...$var..."` form.

## Try this

- Evaluate `(1 to 10).filter(_ % 2 == 0)`.
- Try `List("b", "a", "c").sorted`.
- Compute `(1 to 5).product`.

## Going further

The official tour and docs: https://docs.scala-lang.org/

spaces.name: Julia
spaces.blurb: Reads like Python, runs like C — a scientific-computing language built for fast numerical work.

files.content:
println("Hello, Julia!")

squares = [x^2 for x in 1:5]
println("Squares: ", squares)

total = sum(1:100)
println("Sum 1..100 = ", total)

spaces.tutorial:
# Julia

Julia was designed to solve the "two-language problem": prototype in a high-level language, then rewrite the hot loops in C. Julia lets you do both at once — it reads like Python but its just-in-time compiler produces code that rivals C. It shines in numerical, scientific, and data-heavy work, with array math and broadcasting built in.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch `julia`. The prompt is `julia>`. Expressions evaluate and print their result immediately. Try these in order:

```julia
1 + 2
sum(1:100)
[x^2 for x in 1:5]
uppercase("hello")
map(x -> x * x, 1:5)
```

Gotchas: arrays and ranges are **1-indexed**. `1:100` is an inclusive range, and math is *vectorized* — many functions like `sum` accept a range directly. The `[expr for x in ...]` form is a comprehension. The very first call in a session may pause briefly while Julia compiles; subsequent calls are fast.

## The sample

Open `hello.jl`. It greets you with `println`, builds `squares` using a comprehension `[x^2 for x in 1:5]`, prints them, then sums `1:100` with the built-in `sum`.

## Try this

- Evaluate `filter(iseven, 1:10)`.
- Try broadcasting: `(1:5) .^ 2` (note the dot).
- Compute `prod(1:5)` (factorial of 5).

## Going further

The official manual: https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/getting-started/

spaces.blurb: Python's readability compiled to C's speed — metaprogramming so good the stdlib is built on it.

snippets: for i in 1..3: echo i * i

#   nim c -r hello.nim

spaces.tutorial:
# Nim

Nim compiles to C, so it runs at systems speed — but reads like a
scripting language, and its macro system rewrites the AST at compile
time (much of the standard library is built with it).

## Your REPL

Click **START** and the studio runs `nim secret` — the compiler's
interactive VM. It executes Nim at the prompt (a hidden gem: it ships
with every Nim install).

## The sample

`hello.nim` shows procs and uniform function call syntax —
`greet("Nim")` and `"Nim".greet()` are the same call, which is how
Nim gets method-chaining without classes.

## Try

1. In the REPL: `echo 2 + 3`, then `for i in 1..3: echo i * i`.
2. Compile the sample: rack IGNITION on a nimble project runs
   `nimble run`; or in a terminal `nim c -r hello.nim`.
3. Break it on purpose: change `& name` to `& 42` and read the
   compile error — Nim's type errors name both sides.

---

## Install

If the tool isn't found when you press START, install it:

```sh
brew install nim
```

spaces.name: V
spaces.blurb: Compiles itself in under a second into small, dependency-free native binaries — Go's ergonomics with a batteries-included web story (vweb).
spaces.tutorial:
# V

V is a statically-typed language that compiles itself in under a
second and produces small, dependency-free native binaries. Its syntax
reads like Go with the sharp edges filed off, and `vweb` gives it a
batteries-included web story.

## Your REPL

Click **START** and the studio runs `v repl` — type an expression and
V compiles-and-runs each line.

## The sample

`hello.v` is a whole V program: a `module main`, a typed `greet`
function using string interpolation (`${name}`), and `main`.

## Try

1. In the REPL: `println(2 + 3)`, then `println('v'.to_upper())`.
2. Run the sample: rack IGNITION runs `v run .`; or in a terminal
   `v run hello.v`.
3. Build a native binary: `v .` drops an executable beside the
   source — no runtime, no dependencies.

---

## Troubleshooting (macOS/Homebrew)

If compilation fails with `ld: library 'gc' not found`, Homebrew's V
sometimes misses the brew lib path. Point it there once:

```sh
v -cflags "-L$(brew --prefix)/lib" run hello.v
```

(or add `export LDFLAGS=-L$(brew --prefix)/lib` to your shell.)

---

## Install

If the tool isn't found when you press START, install it:

```sh
brew install vlang
```

spaces.name: Fortran
spaces.blurb: The original high-performance language — still the backbone of scientific and HPC computing, now with a modern package manager (fpm).

install.linux: sudo apt install gfortran && see fpm.fortran-lang.org

files.content:
name = "hello"
version = "0.1.0"

program hello
  implicit none
  print *, greet("Fortran")
  print *, "first five squares:", squares(5)
contains
  function greet(name) result(msg)
    character(len=*), intent(in) :: name
    character(len=:), allocatable :: msg
    msg = "Hello, " // name // "!"
  end function greet

  function squares(n) result(out)
    integer, intent(in) :: n
    integer :: out(n), i
    do i = 1, n
      out(i) = i * i
    end do
  end function squares
end program hello

spaces.tutorial:
# Fortran

Fortran (Formula Translation) is the oldest high-level language still
in wide use — and for good reason: it remains the fastest, most
trusted tool for numerical and high-performance computing, from
weather models to linear-algebra libraries. Modern Fortran (2008/2018)
is a clean, array-oriented language, and **fpm** (the Fortran Package
Manager) finally gives it a cargo-like build/run/test story.

## Your REPL

Fortran has no standard REPL, so this space uses **run** mode. Click
**RUN** to build and run the project with `fpm run`. Edit
`app/main.f90`, click RUN again, and see your changes.

## The sample

`fpm.toml` is the whole manifest — a name and a version. `app/main.f90`
is a `program` with two internal `function`s (after `contains`):
`greet` shows character-string handling, `squares` shows Fortran's
native array returns.

## Try

1. Click RUN — fpm compiles and runs, printing the greeting and the
   squares array.
2. Change `squares(5)` to `squares(8)` and RUN again.
3. Add a `real :: pi = 3.14159` and `print *, pi` to see Fortran's
   numeric types.

---

## Install

If `fpm` isn't found when you press RUN, install it (you also need a
Fortran compiler like gfortran):

```sh
brew install fpm gfortran
```

spaces.tutorial:
# GNU Smalltalk

Smalltalk is where objects, classes, IDEs, and live coding were born —
everything is an object, all computation is messages, and the system is
alive while you shape it. `gst` is the GNU implementation: Smalltalk as
a scripting language, no image browser required.

## Your REPL

Click **START** and the studio runs `gst`. Every line you type is a
message send — `(3 + 4) printNl.` sends `+` to `3`, then `printNl` to
the result.

## The sample

`hello.st` defines a class the gst way — `Object subclass: Greeter [...]`
with a `greet:` method — then instantiates and messages it.

## Try

1. `(3 + 4) printNl.` then `'hello' asUppercase printNl.`
2. Blocks are objects too: `#(1 2 3) collect: [:x | x * x].`
3. Everything is an object: `3 class printNl.` then `3 class class printNl.`

Note: Smalltalk has no line comment — comments are "double-quoted" —
so the editor's comment-toggle stays off in .st files on purpose.

---

## Install

```sh
brew install gnu-smalltalk
```

spaces.name: Prolog
spaces.blurb: Programming as logic: state facts and rules, ask questions, and let unification do the work — SWI-Prolog is the modern standard.

files.content:
% Load me in the REPL with:  ?- ['family.pro'].
% (or run: swipl -g main -t halt family.pro)
parent(tom, bob).
parent(tom, liz).
parent(bob, ann).
parent(bob, pat).

grandparent(X, Z) :- parent(X, Y), parent(Y, Z).

main :-
    forall(grandparent(G, C),
           format("~w is a grandparent of ~w~n", [G, C])).

spaces.tutorial:
# Prolog

Prolog turns programming inside out: you state *facts* and *rules*,
then ask *questions* — the engine searches for every way to make them
true. It still powers scheduling, parsing, and knowledge systems, and
SWI-Prolog is the batteries-included modern implementation.

## Your REPL

Click **START** and the studio runs `swipl`. Every query ends with a
period. `member(X, [1,2,3]).` answers `X = 1` — type `;` for more
solutions.

## The sample

`family.pro` is the classic: `parent` facts and a `grandparent` rule
that *derives* new knowledge. Load it with `['family.pro'].` then ask
`grandparent(tom, Who).`

## Try

1. `member(X, [1,2,3]).` — then `;` to enumerate.
2. `length(L, 3).` — Prolog runs backwards: it *builds* a list.
3. `between(1, 5, X), Y is X*X.` — generate and compute.

Note: .pl belongs to Perl in this IDE — name Prolog files .pro or
.prolog and they get full Prolog highlighting.

---

## Install

```sh
brew install swi-prolog
```

# Tcl

Tcl ("tickle") is radical minimalism: every line is a command, every
value is a string, and the whole language fits in twelve rules. That
simplicity made it the embeddable scripting layer everywhere — Tk GUIs,
expect, network gear, and nearly every chip-design (EDA) tool.

## Your REPL

Click **START** and the studio runs `tclsh`. `puts [expr {2 + 3}]`
prints 5 — square brackets substitute a command's result, `$name`
substitutes a variable.

## The sample

`hello.tcl` shows a `proc`, string interpolation, and building a list
with `lappend` in a `foreach`.

## Try

1. `set name "Tcl"; puts "Hello, $name!"`
2. `proc double {x} { return [expr {$x * 2}] }; puts [double 21]`
3. `info commands l*` — the language can inspect itself.

brew install tcl-tk

# Scheme (Guile)

Scheme is the minimalist Lisp — the language of SICP, where closures,
recursion, and code-as-data are taught in their purest form. GNU Guile
is its workhorse implementation: the official extension language of the
GNU project (GDB, Guix, and more embed it).

expression: `(+ 1 2)` evaluates to 3.

## The sample

over `(iota 5 1)` — Scheme's range.

and `.scm`/`.ss` files get Scheme highlighting while `.rkt` stays Racket.

spaces.tutorial:
# Racket

Racket is the language-oriented programming language: a modern Scheme
whose superpower is building *new* languages — every file declares its
dialect with `#lang`, and the ecosystem ships dozens.

## Your REPL

Click **START** and the studio runs `racket -i` — the interactive
read-eval-print loop (the `-i` matters: piped stdin needs it).

## The sample

`main.rkt` is classic Racket: `#lang racket`, a `define`, and a
`for` comprehension driving `cond`.

## Try

1. `(+ 1 2)` then `(map sqr '(1 2 3))` in the REPL.
2. Define and call: `(define (double x) (* 2 x))` then `(double 21)`.
3. Run the file: IGNITION on a racket project runs `racket main.rkt`.
4. Peek at the language-making soul: change `#lang racket` to
   `#lang slideshow` in a new file and evaluate `(circle 30)`.

---

## Install

If the tool isn't found when you press START, install it:

```sh
brew install minimal-racket
```

# Elm

Elm compiles to JavaScript and famously has **no runtime exceptions** —
if it compiles, it doesn't crash. Its compiler errors read like a
patient teammate explaining what to fix.

## Your REPL

Click **START** and the studio runs `elm repl`. Try the snippets — the
REPL prints the *type* of every result beside its value, which is half
the Elm experience.

## The sample

`src/Main.elm` is the smallest real Elm app: a typed `greeting`
function rendered with `Html.text`.

## Try

2. `List.map (\x -> x * x) [1, 2, 3]`.

spaces.name: R
spaces.blurb: The statistician's language, where whole vectors are the unit of thought and plots come free.

files.content:
cat("Hello, R!\n")

squares <- sapply(1:5, function(x) x^2)
cat("Squares:", squares, "\n")

total <- sum(1:100)
cat("Sum 1..100 =", total, "\n")

spaces.tutorial:
# R

R is the lingua franca of statistics and data analysis. It was built by statisticians for statisticians, so vectors, data frames, and plots are first-class citizens. If you need a summary, a regression, or a chart, R usually gets you there in a line or two.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch `R --no-save --quiet`. The prompt is `>`. R is deeply **vectorized**: `1:100` is a whole vector, and most functions operate on entire vectors at once — no loop needed. Try these in order:

```r
1 + 2
sum(1:100)
sapply(1:5, function(x) x^2)
toupper("hello")
mean(c(2, 4, 6, 8))
```

Gotchas: assignment traditionally uses `<-` (though `=` also works). `c(...)` combines values into a vector. Because everything is vectorized, `sapply` applies a function over each element and returns a vector. Type `q()` to quit (we launched with `--no-save` so it won't nag you about the workspace).

## The sample

Open `hello.R`. It prints with `cat` (which does not auto-append a newline, so we add `"\n"`), computes `squares` by applying `x^2` over `1:5` with `sapply`, and totals `1:100` with `sum`.

## Try this

- Evaluate `seq(0, 1, by = 0.25)`.
- Try `summary(c(3, 7, 7, 19))`.
- Compute `sd(1:10)` (standard deviation).

## Going further

The official introduction: https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-intro.html

spaces.name: Perl
spaces.blurb: The original swiss-army chainsaw of text processing, where regular expressions are a native tongue.
spaces.tutorial:
# Perl

Perl earned its reputation as the duct tape of the internet: unmatched at slicing, dicing, and reshaping text, with regular expressions woven right into the language. It glues systems together, powers countless sysadmin scripts, and still ships on nearly every Unix box.

## Your REPL

Perl has no polished REPL out of the box. This space is set up in **run** mode: click **START** and it runs `perl hello.pl` and shows the output. (If you want to poke around interactively outside this space, `perl -de1` opens Perl's debugger as a makeshift REPL — but it's quirky, so we keep it honest and just run files here.)

Edit `hello.pl` and press **START** again to see your changes.

## The sample

Open `hello.pl`. The `use strict; use warnings;` pragmas at the top are the sign of well-behaved modern Perl — turn them on always. Then:

```perl
my @squares = map { $_ * $_ } (1 .. 5);
```

The `@` sigil marks an array, `$_` is the implicit loop variable, and `map { ... }` transforms each element. The sum is built with a statement modifier: `$total += $_ for (1 .. 100);` reads right-to-left as "add each number to total." Note that arrays interpolate directly inside double-quoted strings (`"@squares"`).

## Try this

- Change `(1 .. 5)` to `(1 .. 10)` and re-run.
- Add `my @rev = reverse @squares; print "@rev\n";`.
- Try a regex: `my $s = "hello world"; $s =~ s/world/Perl/; print "$s\n";`.

## Going further

The official docs and tutorials: https://www.perl.org/docs.html

spaces.name: Bash
spaces.blurb: The command line you already live in — a full programming language wearing a prompt.

snippets: for i in 1 2 3; do echo $i; done

files.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

echo "Hello, Bash!"

for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do
  echo "$i squared = $(( i * i ))"
done

total=0
for i in $(seq 1 100); do
  total=$(( total + i ))
done
echo "Sum 1..100 = $total"

spaces.tutorial:
# Bash

Bash is the shell most Unix systems drop you into, and it's a real programming language: variables, loops, conditionals, functions, and — its superpower — pipelines that stitch small tools into big results. Learning Bash means learning to automate everything you do at the terminal.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch an interactive `bash` shell. The prompt is `$`. A shell *is* a REPL — every line you type runs immediately. Try these in order:

```bash
echo $((2 + 2))
for i in 1 2 3; do echo $i; done
echo hello | tr a-z A-Z
seq 1 100 | paste -sd+ | bc
ls | wc -l
```

Gotchas: arithmetic goes inside `$(( ... ))`. The pipe `|` sends one command's output into the next — `seq 1 100 | paste -sd+ | bc` builds the string `1+2+...+100` and feeds it to the calculator `bc`. Quoting matters: use `"$var"` to keep spaces intact.

## The sample

Open `hello.sh`. The shebang `#!/usr/bin/env bash` picks the interpreter, and `set -euo pipefail` is the safety belt (exit on error, on unset variables, and on failed pipes). It then loops 1..5 printing each square with `$(( i * i ))`, and accumulates a `total` over `$(seq 1 100)`.

## Try this

- In the shell: `date` and then `whoami`.
- Save a variable: `name=world; echo "hi $name"`.
- Count words: `echo "one two three" | wc -w`.

## Going further

The GNU Bash reference manual: https://www.gnu.org/software/bash/manual/bash.html

spaces.name: SQLite
spaces.blurb: The world's most-deployed database, a whole SQL engine in one file, ready in your terminal.

snippets: SELECT x, x * x AS sq FROM nums ORDER BY x;

spaces.tutorial:
# SQLite

SQLite is the most widely deployed database engine on Earth — it lives inside your phone, your browser, and countless apps. There's no server to run: the whole database is a single file (or, here, purely in memory). It speaks standard SQL, so this space is really a place to learn SQL itself.

## Your REPL

Click **START** to launch `sqlite3 :memory:`, an in-memory database that vanishes when you're done — perfect for experimenting. The prompt is `sqlite>`. The one rule that trips everyone up: **every statement ends with a semicolon `;`**. Without it, SQLite shows a continuation prompt `...>` and waits. Try these in order:

```sql
SELECT 1 + 1;
CREATE TABLE nums(x INTEGER);
INSERT INTO nums VALUES (5), (7), (9);
SELECT sum(x) FROM nums;
SELECT x, x * x AS sq FROM nums ORDER BY x;
```

They run in order: create a table, put three numbers in it, then query it. Dot-commands like `.tables` and `.schema` (no semicolon) inspect the database, and `.quit` exits.

## The sample

Open `schema.sql`. It creates a `fruit` table with a text `name` and a real `price`, inserts three rows in one statement, then runs two queries: one ordering fruit by price descending, and one aggregating a `count(*)` and a rounded `sum(price)` with column aliases via `AS`.

## Try this

- `SELECT name FROM fruit WHERE price < 1.0;`
- `SELECT avg(price) FROM fruit;`
- Add a row: `INSERT INTO fruit VALUES ('date', 3.5);` then re-run the total.

## Going further

The official SQL syntax reference: https://www.sqlite.org/lang.html

spaces.name: React
spaces.blurb: You build a UI from components and state; React re-renders the screen when the state changes.
spaces.tutorial:
# React

React lets you describe a UI as a tree of components. Each component returns markup (JSX) built from its **props** (inputs) and **state** (memory). When state changes, React re-renders just what changed.

## Get it running

1. Install Node (see the install hints for your OS).
2. In the terminal, run `npm install` once to pull `react`, `react-dom`, and `vite`.
3. Click **RUN** (this runs `npm run dev`). Vite prints a `http://localhost:5173` URL. Open it.

You'll see a heading and a counter button. Vite hot-reloads the page every time you save a file.

## The shape

- `index.html` — the single page. It has one empty `<div id="root">` and loads `src/main.jsx`.
- `src/main.jsx` — the bootstrap. `createRoot(...).render(<App />)` mounts your component tree into that div.
- `src/App.jsx` — your first component. `useState(0)` gives you a `count` value and a `setCount` updater. Clicking the button calls `setCount`, which triggers a re-render with the new number.

That's the whole mental model: **state in, markup out, re-render on change.**

## Try this

1. Add a second button that resets the count: `<button onClick={() => setCount(0)}>Reset</button>`.
2. Change the heading based on state: `{count > 4 ? "That's a lot of clicks" : "Hello, React"}`.
3. Extract the button into its own component `Counter` that takes `count` and `onClick` as props, and render it from `App`.

## Going further

Official interactive tutorial and docs: https://react.dev/learn

spaces.name: Vue
spaces.blurb: You write single-file components with reactive state, and Vue keeps the DOM in sync automatically.
spaces.tutorial:
# Vue

Vue centers on the **single-file component** (`.vue`): script, template, and (optionally) style live in one file. State declared with `ref()` is *reactive* — when its value changes, Vue updates the DOM for you.

## Get it running

1. Install Node.
2. Run `npm install` once to pull `vue` and `vite`.
3. Click **RUN** (`npm run dev`). Open the printed `http://localhost:5173` URL.

A counter appears. Saving any file hot-reloads the page.

## The shape

- `index.html` — one empty `<div id="app">` and a script that loads `src/main.js`.
- `src/main.js` — `createApp(App).mount("#app")` mounts your root component.
- `src/App.vue` — the component. `<script setup>` holds logic; `count = ref(0)` is reactive state. `<template>` is the markup — `{{ count }}` interpolates the value and `@click="count++"` wires the event. No `setState` call needed; mutating `count.value` (or `count` in the template) is enough.
- `vite.config.js` — registers the Vue plugin so Vite can compile `.vue` files.

## Try this

1. Add a reset button: `<button @click="count = 0">Reset</button>`.
2. Add a computed message. In `<script setup>`: `import { computed } from "vue"` then `const msg = computed(() => count.value > 4 ? "Lots!" : "Hello")`, and show `{{ msg }}`.
3. Add an input bound with `v-model` to a new `ref` and echo it below.

## Going further

Official guide and interactive tutorial: https://vuejs.org/guide/introduction.html

spaces.name: Svelte
spaces.blurb: You write plain-looking components; Svelte compiles them to tiny, fast code with no virtual DOM.

files.content:
Hello, Svelte

You clicked {count} times.

spaces.tutorial:
# Svelte

Svelte is a **compiler**, not a runtime library. You write components that look like plain HTML + JS, and Svelte compiles them at build time into small, surgical DOM updates — there's no virtual DOM diffing at runtime.

## Get it running

1. Install Node.
2. Run `npm install` once to pull `svelte` and `vite`.
3. Click **RUN** (`npm run dev`). Open the printed `http://localhost:5173` URL.

A counter appears; saves hot-reload.

## The shape

- `index.html` — an empty `<div id="app">` plus a script loading `src/main.js`.
- `src/main.js` — `new App({ target: ... })` instantiates the root component into that div.
- `src/App.svelte` — the component. Reactivity is *assignment-based*: because `count` is a top-level `let`, writing `count++` tells Svelte to update the DOM. No hooks, no `ref`, no `this.setState`.
- `vite.config.js` — registers the Svelte plugin so Vite compiles `.svelte` files.

## Try this

1. Add a reset button: `<button on:click={() => (count = 0)}>Reset</button>`.
2. Add a *reactive statement*: `$: doubled = count * 2;` then show `{doubled}` in the markup — it recomputes whenever `count` changes.
3. Add an `{#if count > 4}Lots of clicks!{/if}` block to show conditional markup.

## Going further

Official interactive tutorial (highly recommended): https://svelte.dev/tutorial

spaces.name: Angular
spaces.blurb: A full, opinionated TypeScript framework: components, dependency injection, and tooling all included.

install.linux: sudo apt-get install -y nodejs npm && npm install -g @angular/cli

spaces.tutorial:
# Angular

Angular is a **full, batteries-included** framework built on TypeScript. Unlike the lighter libraries, it ships routing, forms, HTTP, dependency injection, and a build system as one opinionated whole — and it leans on its own CLI (`ng`) for almost everything.

## Honest note: this is a starting point

Angular really wants to be scaffolded by its CLI. The files here are a minimal standalone-component sketch to read, but the cleanest path to a *fully wired* project is to let the CLI generate it:

```
npm install -g @angular/cli
ng new angular-space --minimal --defaults
cd angular-space
```

That produces the full `angular.json`, `tsconfig`, and build config a real app needs.

## Get it running

1. Install Node and the Angular CLI (see install hints).
2. Run `npm install`.
3. Click **START** (this runs `npm start` -> `ng serve`). Open `http://localhost:4200`.

If `ng serve` complains about missing config, run the `ng new` scaffold above and copy your component into it — that's the honest, supported route.

## The shape

- `src/main.ts` — the entry point. `bootstrapApplication(AppComponent)` boots a standalone component (no NgModule needed in modern Angular). The `@Component` decorator ties a `selector`, an inline `template`, and a class together. `signal(0)` is Angular's reactive state; `count()` reads it, `count.set(...)` writes it.
- `src/index.html` — hosts `<app-root>`, where the component renders.

Core idea: **components + decorators + dependency injection**, all typed.

## Try this

1. Add a reset button: `<button (click)="count.set(0)">Reset</button>`.
2. Add a `computed`: `import { computed } from "@angular/core"` then `doubled = computed(() => this.count() * 2);` and show `{{ doubled() }}`.
3. Scaffold a real project with `ng new` and run `ng generate component hello` to see Angular's file conventions.

## Going further

Official tutorial: https://angular.dev/tutorials/learn-angular

spaces.name: Next.js
spaces.blurb: React with a filesystem router, server rendering, and a build system wired together for you.
spaces.tutorial:
# Next.js

Next.js is React plus the plumbing a real app needs: a **filesystem router**, server rendering, bundling, and API routes — all wired together. In the App Router, folders under `app/` become URLs.

## Get it running

1. Install Node.
2. Run `npm install` to pull `next`, `react`, and `react-dom`.
3. Click **RUN** (`npm run dev`). Open `http://localhost:3000`.

Edits hot-reload. (The first request compiles on demand, so it can be a beat slow.)

## The shape

Routing is the file tree:

- `app/layout.jsx` — the root layout wrapping every page. It renders `<html>` and `<body>` and receives each page as `children`.
- `app/page.jsx` — the home route, `/`. Components here are **Server Components** by default: they run on the server and ship as HTML, which is why there's no `useState` in this file.
- `app/about/page.jsx` — because it's in the `about/` folder, its URL is `/about`. No route config to write.
- `<Link>` from `next/link` does client-side navigation between them.

Core idea: **the folder structure *is* the router**, and rendering happens on the server unless you opt a component into the client.

## Try this

1. Add a new route: create `app/blog/page.jsx` and visit `/blog`.
2. Make a client component: add `"use client";` at the top of a new component file and use `useState` for a counter, then render it from a page.
3. Add a dynamic route: `app/blog/[slug]/page.jsx` and read `params.slug` from its props.

## Going further

Official learn course and docs: https://nextjs.org/learn

spaces.name: Express
spaces.blurb: A tiny Node HTTP framework: you map routes to handlers and return JSON in a few lines.
spaces.tutorial:
# Express

Express is a minimal HTTP framework for Node. You attach **routes** (method + path) to **handler functions**, and each handler gets a `req` (request) and `res` (response). That's essentially the whole API.

## Get it running

1. Install Node.
2. Run `npm install` to pull `express`.
3. Click **RUN** (`node server.js`). You'll see `Listening on http://localhost:3000`.

Open `http://localhost:3000/api/todos` in a browser to see JSON.

### Poke it with PING

Because Express serves plain HTTP, you can point the **PING** device at `http://localhost:3000/api/todos` to watch the endpoint respond without leaving the IDE. Great for confirming the server is live.

## The shape

- `package.json` — declares the `express` dependency.
- `server.js` — the whole app:
  - `express.json()` middleware parses JSON request bodies into `req.body`.
  - `app.get("/api/todos", ...)` returns the in-memory `todos` array as JSON.
  - `app.post("/api/todos", ...)` reads `req.body.text`, pushes a new todo, and responds `201 Created`.
  - `app.listen(PORT, ...)` binds the server to a port.

Core idea: **route -> handler -> response**, with middleware in between.

## Try this

1. Add `app.get("/api/todos/:id", ...)` and read `req.params.id` to return a single todo.
2. Add a `DELETE /api/todos/:id` route that removes a todo by id.
3. Add simple validation: if `req.body.text` is missing on POST, respond `res.status(400).json({ error: "text required" })`.

## Going further

Official guide: https://expressjs.com/en/starter/basic-routing.html

spaces.blurb: Python's batteries-included web framework: an ORM, admin, and routing that scales from prototype to production.

This space is a STARTING POINT. Django wants to be scaffolded by its own tool.

Run these once, in this folder, before the driver will work:

  python3 -m pip install -r requirements.txt

spaces.tutorial:
# Django

Django is Python's **batteries-included** web framework: it ships an ORM (database models as Python classes), an auto-generated admin site, a URL router, templating, and migrations. It has a strong "the framework decides the layout" philosophy.

## Honest note: this is a starting point

Django is generated by its own command, `django-admin`. This space can't ship a full working project as loose files, so you run one scaffold step first. In this folder:

```
python3 -m pip install -r requirements.txt
django-admin startproject site .
python3 manage.py migrate
```

That creates `manage.py`, a `site/settings.py`, and a SQLite DB. `README-first.txt` has the same steps.

## Get it running

After the scaffold above you have two ways in:

- **A dev server** — run `python3 manage.py runserver` and open `http://localhost:8000` for the Django welcome page.
- **The REPL** — the driver here is `python3 manage.py shell`, an interactive Python shell with your project fully loaded. Click **RUN** to open it, then try the snippets. This is the richest way to explore models and the ORM.

## The shape

- `manage.py` (generated) — the command runner for everything: `runserver`, `migrate`, `shell`, `createsuperuser`.
- `site/settings.py` (generated) — one place for database, apps, and config.
- The **shell** is your playground. `User.objects.count()` runs a real ORM query; `django.get_version()` confirms your install.

Core idea: **models describe your data, the ORM queries it, and management commands drive everything.**

## Try this

1. In the shell: `from django.contrib.auth.models import User` then `User.objects.create_user("alice")` and re-check `User.objects.count()`.
2. Create an app: `python3 manage.py startapp blog`, then define a model in `blog/models.py`.
3. Run `python3 manage.py createsuperuser`, start the server, and log in at `/admin`.

## Going further

The official tutorial builds a real poll app step by step: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/stable/intro/tutorial01/

spaces.name: Flask
spaces.blurb: A tiny Python web framework: decorate a function with a route and you have a running endpoint.

install.mac: brew install python && python3 -m pip install flask
install.linux: sudo apt-get install -y python3 python3-pip && python3 -m pip install flask
install.windows: winget install Python.Python.3.12 && python -m pip install flask

spaces.tutorial:
# Flask

Flask is a **micro** web framework for Python. It gives you routing and request handling and gets out of the way — you add only the pieces you need. A route is just a function with a decorator.

## Get it running

1. Install Python and pip.
2. Run `python3 -m pip install -r requirements.txt`.
3. Click **RUN** (`flask --app app run`). You'll see it serving on `http://localhost:5000`.

Open `http://localhost:5000/api/todos` to see JSON. (You can also just run `python3 app.py` — the `__main__` block starts the same server with debug reload.)

## The shape

- `requirements.txt` — the single dependency, Flask.
- `app.py` — the whole app:
  - `app = Flask(__name__)` creates the application.
  - `@app.get("/api/todos")` binds a URL to a function; whatever it returns becomes the response. `jsonify(...)` serializes Python lists/dicts to JSON.
  - `@app.post("/api/todos")` reads the JSON body via `request.get_json()`, appends a todo, and returns it with a `201` status.

Core idea: **decorate a function with a route, return a value, done.**

## Try this

1. Add a route with a URL parameter: `@app.get("/api/todos/<int:id>")` and return the matching todo.
2. Add a `@app.delete("/api/todos/<int:id>")` route to remove one.
3. Render HTML instead of JSON: return an f-string of `<ul>` list items from `/`.

## Going further

The official quickstart builds on exactly this: https://flask.palletsprojects.com/en/stable/quickstart/

spaces.name: Ruby on Rails
spaces.blurb: Convention-over-configuration Ruby: generators, an ORM, and MVC that turn ideas into apps fast.

install.windows: winget install RubyInstallerTeam.Ruby.3.3 && gem install rails

files.content:
source "https://rubygems.org"

gem "rails", "~> 7.1"

This space is a STARTING POINT. Rails apps are generated by the `rails` command.

Install Ruby, then the Rails gem, then scaffold IN THIS FOLDER:

  gem install rails
  rails new . --minimal
  bin/rails db:create db:migrate

That generates the full app skeleton (app/, config/, bin/rails, a database, etc.).

After that:
  - RUN a dev server:   bin/rails server        (http://localhost:3000)
  - Open the console:   bin/rails console       (the driver here)

spaces.tutorial:
# Ruby on Rails

Rails is the original **convention-over-configuration** framework: follow its naming and folder conventions and it wires controllers, models, views, and routes together for you. Generators scaffold whole features from one command.

## Honest note: this is a starting point

A Rails app is *generated*, not shipped as loose files. This space gives you a `Gemfile` and instructions; you run one scaffold step. In this folder:

```
gem install rails
rails new . --minimal
bin/rails db:create db:migrate
```

That creates the full `app/`, `config/`, `bin/rails`, and a SQLite database. `README-first.txt` repeats these steps.

## Get it running

Once scaffolded, two doors:

- **A dev server** — `bin/rails server`, then open `http://localhost:3000`.
- **The console** — the driver here is `bin/rails console`, an IRB session with your whole app loaded (models, config, the DB). Click **RUN** and try the snippets. It's the fastest way to learn Rails' ORM, ActiveRecord.

## The shape

- `Gemfile` — declares Rails; `bundle install` (run by `rails new`) resolves gems.
- `bin/rails` (generated) — the command hub: `server`, `console`, `generate`, `db:migrate`.
- The **console** is your lab. `Rails.version` confirms the install; `ActiveRecord::Base.connection.tables` lists your database tables.

Core idea: **conventions + generators + ActiveRecord** mean you write features, not boilerplate.

2. In the console: `Post.create(title: "Hello", body: "World")` then `Post.count` and `Post.first`.
3. Start the server and visit `/posts` to see the generated CRUD UI.

# Phoenix

Phoenix is the web framework for **Elixir**, which runs on the Erlang VM (BEAM). That heritage gives it real superpowers: cheap concurrency, fault tolerance, and real-time features (LiveView) over WebSockets that feel effortless.

## Honest note: this is a starting point

Phoenix apps are generated by the `phx.new` task, so this space ships instructions plus an *example* `mix.exs` to read (the real one is generated). In this folder:

```
mix local.hex --force
mix archive.install hex phx_new --force
mix phx.new . --app my_app --no-ecto
mix deps.get
```

That writes the real `mix.exs`, `lib/`, router, and endpoint. `README-first.txt` has the same steps.

## Get it running

After scaffolding, two ways in:

- **A dev server** — `mix phx.server`, then open `http://localhost:4000`.
- **The REPL** — the driver here is `iex -S mix`: an interactive Elixir shell with your app started. Click **RUN** and try the snippets. This is *the* way to explore Elixir and your running app; you can even call your own modules live.

## The shape

- `mix.exs` (generated) — the project + dependency manifest; `mix` is Elixir's build tool.
- `lib/my_app_web/` (generated) — router, controllers, and LiveViews.
- The **IEx** session is your lab. `System.version()` shows your Elixir version; `Enum.map(1..5, & &1 * &1)` shows Elixir's functional style.

Core idea: **the BEAM's concurrency + Phoenix's router/LiveView** give you real-time apps with little ceremony.

## Try this

1. In IEx: `Enum.filter(1..10, fn n -> rem(n, 2) == 0 end)` to see pipelines and anonymous functions.
2. Generate a LiveView: `mix phx.gen.live Blog Post posts title:string`.
3. Start `mix phx.server` and edit a template under `lib/my_app_web/` — the browser live-reloads.

## Going further

Official guides (start with "Up and Running"): https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix/overview.html

# Spring Boot

Spring Boot is the mainstream way to build web apps and APIs in **Java**. It takes the powerful-but-heavy Spring framework and adds *auto-configuration* plus an embedded server, so one annotated class becomes a running web service.

## Honest note: you need a JDK and Maven

This space is a real, minimal Maven project, but it needs a **JDK 17+** and **Maven** installed (see the install hints). No scaffold step is required beyond those tools.

## Get it running

1. Install a JDK and Maven.
2. Click **RUN** (this runs `mvn spring-boot:run`). Maven downloads dependencies the first time (be patient), then boots an embedded Tomcat.
3. Open `http://localhost:8080/` and then `http://localhost:8080/greet?name=Ada`.

(If you prefer the wrapper, real projects usually ship `./mvnw`; you can generate one with `mvn wrapper:wrapper`.)

## The shape

- `pom.xml` — the build file. The `spring-boot-starter-parent` and `spring-boot-starter-web` starter pull in a curated, compatible set of libraries so you don't manage versions by hand.
- `Application.java` — the entire app:
  - `@SpringBootApplication` turns on auto-configuration and component scanning.
  - `main(...)` calls `SpringApplication.run(...)`, which starts the embedded server.
  - `@RestController` + `@GetMapping("/greet")` map an HTTP path to a method; the return value becomes the response body. `@RequestParam` binds `?name=` from the URL.

Core idea: **annotations declare intent; Spring wires and runs the rest.**

## Try this

1. Add a JSON endpoint: return a small record like `record Greeting(String message) {}` from a new `@GetMapping` and Spring serializes it to JSON.
2. Add a `@PostMapping` that accepts a `@RequestBody`.
3. Split the controller into its own `@RestController` class to see Spring's component scanning find it.

## Going further

The official "Building a RESTful Web Service" guide mirrors this: https://spring.io/guides/gs/rest-service

# Laravel

Laravel is PHP's most popular framework, known for **developer ergonomics**: clean routing, the expressive Eloquent ORM, Blade templates, queues, and a friendly CLI (`artisan`). It also ships a delightful REPL, **Tinker**.

## Honest note: this is a starting point

Laravel is created by Composer, so this space ships instructions plus a preview of `routes/web.php`. In this (empty) folder:

```
composer create-project laravel/laravel .
```

That downloads Laravel and generates `artisan`, `app/`, `routes/`, and a `.env`. `README-first.txt` repeats the steps.

## Get it running

After scaffolding, two ways in:

- **A dev server** — the RUN command style is `php artisan serve`; open `http://localhost:8000`.
- **Tinker (the REPL)** — the driver here is `php artisan tinker`, an interactive shell with your whole app booted. Click **RUN** and try the snippets. You can call Eloquent models, helpers, and your own classes live. It's the best way to learn the framework's API.

## The shape

- `routes/web.php` (generated) — maps URLs to closures or controllers. `Route::get('/greet/{name}', ...)` shows a route parameter.
- `artisan` (generated) — the CLI hub: `serve`, `tinker`, `make:model`, `migrate`.
- **Tinker** is your lab. `now()` returns a Carbon date; `collect([1,2,3])->map(...)` shows Laravel's fluent collections; `app()->version()` confirms the install.

Core idea: **expressive helpers + Eloquent + artisan** make common web tasks short and readable.

## Try this

1. In Tinker: `Str::upper('laravel');` and `collect(['a','b','c'])->reverse()->all();`.
2. Make a model: `php artisan make:model Post -m`, edit the migration, then `php artisan migrate`.
3. In Tinker: `App\Models\Post::create(['title' => 'Hi']);` then `App\Models\Post::count();`.

## Going further

Official docs (start with Routing and Eloquent): https://laravel.com/docs

# ASP.NET

ASP.NET (Core) is Microsoft's cross-platform web framework, written in **C#** and running on the .NET runtime. Modern **minimal APIs** let you build a real HTTP service in a handful of lines — `app.MapGet(...)` maps a route to a lambda.

## Honest note: you need the .NET SDK

This is a real, minimal project, but it needs the **.NET SDK** installed (see install hints). No extra scaffold step is required — `dotnet run` builds and runs it.

## Get it running

1. Install the .NET SDK.
2. Click **RUN** (this runs `dotnet run`). The first run restores packages and compiles; then Kestrel (the built-in server) starts.
3. The console prints a URL like `http://localhost:5000`. Open it, then try `/greet/Ada` and `/api/todos`.

## The shape

- `aspnet-space.csproj` — the project file. `Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web"` pulls in the web framework; `ImplicitUsings` and `Nullable` are modern C# conveniences.
- `Program.cs` — the whole app, top to bottom:
  - `WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args)` then `.Build()` sets up the host.
  - `app.MapGet("/greet/{name}", (string name) => ...)` binds a route with a parameter straight to a lambda; the return value becomes the response.
  - `app.MapPost("/api/todos", (Todo todo) => ...)` model-binds the JSON body to a `Todo` record and returns `201 Created`.
  - `record Todo(...)` is a compact immutable data type.

Core idea: **map routes to lambdas; the framework handles binding, serialization, and hosting.**

## Try this

1. Add `app.MapGet("/api/todos/{id}", (int id) => todos.FirstOrDefault(t => t.Id == id));`.
2. Return JSON explicitly with `Results.Ok(new { message = "hi" })`.
3. Add a `MapDelete` route that removes a todo by id.

## Going further

Official minimal-API tutorial: https://learn.microsoft.com/aspnet/core/tutorials/min-web-api

import numpy as np

# A 3x5 grid of numbers 0..14
grid = np.arange(15).reshape(3, 5)
print("grid:\n", grid)

# Aggregations across axes
print("column sums:", grid.sum(axis=0))
print("row means:  ", grid.mean(axis=1))

# Boolean masking: keep only even values
print("evens:", grid[grid % 2 == 0])

# Matrix multiply the grid by its transpose
print("grid @ grid.T:\n", grid @ grid.T)

spaces.tutorial:
# NumPy

NumPy is the foundation of scientific Python. It gives you the `ndarray`: a
grid of numbers that supports vectorized math (add two million numbers with
one `+`), broadcasting, slicing, and linear algebra — all running in compiled
C under the hood. Pandas, scikit-learn, and PyTorch all sit on top of it.

## Get set up

You need Python 3 and the library:

```bash
pip3 install numpy
```

## Your REPL

Start Python and try the snippets in order (the import must come first):

```bash
python3
```

```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> a = np.arange(15).reshape(3, 5)
>>> a.sum(axis=0)      # sum down each column
>>> a.mean()           # mean of everything
>>> a @ a.T            # matrix multiply
```

Notice how `a.sum(axis=0)` collapses one dimension — axis 0 is rows, so summing
over it leaves you with one value per column.

## The sample

`stats.py` builds a 3x5 grid, then shows the four things you'll reach for
constantly:

- **aggregation** (`.sum(axis=...)`, `.mean(axis=...)`)
- **boolean masking** (`grid[grid % 2 == 0]` returns only the matching cells)
- **matrix math** (`grid @ grid.T`)

Run it:

```bash
python3 stats.py
```

## Try this

1. Change `axis=0` to `axis=1` and predict the shape before you run it.
2. Make a `10x10` array of random floats with `np.random.rand(10, 10)` and
   find its max with `.max()`.
3. Use `np.linspace(0, 1, 5)` to get 5 evenly spaced points between 0 and 1.

## Going further

The official quickstart is excellent: https://numpy.org/doc/stable/user/quickstart.html

import pandas as pd

# Build a small DataFrame by hand
df = pd.DataFrame({
    "city": ["NYC", "LA", "NYC", "LA", "NYC"],
    "product": ["pen", "pen", "mug", "mug", "pen"],
    "sales": [10, 7, 4, 9, 6],
})
print(df)

# Quick numeric summary
print("\nsummary:\n", df.describe())

# Group and aggregate
print("\nsales per city:\n", df.groupby("city")["sales"].sum())

# Filter rows
print("\nbig sales:\n", df[df.sales > 7])

# Pivot: cities as rows, products as columns
print("\npivot:\n", df.pivot_table(index="city", columns="product", values="sales", aggfunc="sum"))

spaces.tutorial:
# Pandas

Pandas is Python's answer to a spreadsheet or a SQL table. Its central object,
the **DataFrame**, is a 2D table with labeled rows and columns. You use it to
load CSVs, clean messy data, filter and group rows, and reshape data before
analysis or plotting. It's built on NumPy, so it's fast.

## Get set up

```bash
pip3 install pandas
```

## Your REPL

Start Python and run the snippets in order (import first):

```bash
python3
```

```python
>>> import pandas as pd
>>> df = pd.DataFrame({'city': ['NYC', 'LA', 'NYC', 'LA'], 'sales': [10, 7, 4, 9]})
>>> df.describe()                       # count, mean, std, min, max...
>>> df.groupby('city')['sales'].sum()   # split-apply-combine
>>> df[df.sales > 7]                     # boolean row filter
```

The `groupby` line is the heart of Pandas: it splits rows by `city`, then sums
the `sales` in each group.

## The sample

`sales.py` builds a 5-row table of city/product/sales, then demonstrates the
four everyday moves:

- `df.describe()` — numeric summary
- `df.groupby(...)` — aggregate per group
- `df[df.sales > 7]` — filter rows by a condition
- `df.pivot_table(...)` — reshape long data into a matrix

Run it:

```bash
python3 sales.py
```

## Try this

1. Add a `.mean()` aggregation next to the `.sum()` groupby.
2. Sort the DataFrame with `df.sort_values('sales', ascending=False)`.
3. Save it: `df.to_csv('out.csv', index=False)` and reload with
   `pd.read_csv('out.csv')`.

## Going further

Start with the 10-minute tour: https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/user_guide/10min.html

install.mac: brew install python3 && pip3 install torch    # CPU wheel, ~200MB+ download

files.content:
import torch

# A tensor we want gradients for
x = torch.ones(2, requires_grad=True)
print("x:", x)

# Compute a scalar function of x: y = sum(x^2)
y = (x * x).sum()
print("y:", y.item())

# Backprop: fills x.grad with dy/dx = 2x
y.backward()
print("x.grad:", x.grad)   # tensor([2., 2.])

# One step of gradient descent by hand
with torch.no_grad():
    x -= 0.1 * x.grad
print("x after step:", x)

spaces.tutorial:
# PyTorch

PyTorch is a deep-learning framework. Two ideas make it powerful: **tensors**
(NumPy-like arrays that can live on a GPU) and **autograd** (it records the
operations you do and can compute exact gradients by calling `.backward()`).
That's the machinery every neural network trains on.

## Get set up

**Heads up:** PyTorch is a *large* download (the CPU wheel is hundreds of
megabytes). Install the CPU build unless you have a CUDA GPU:

```bash
pip3 install torch
```

On Linux/Windows, pin the CPU index to avoid pulling GPU libraries:

```bash
pip3 install torch --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
```

## Your REPL

Start Python and go in order (import first):

```bash
python3
```

```python
>>> import torch
>>> torch.tensor([1., 2., 3.])
>>> x = torch.ones(2, requires_grad=True)
>>> y = (x * x).sum()
>>> y.backward()      # backprop through y = sum(x^2)
>>> x.grad            # tensor([2., 2.])
```

`requires_grad=True` tells PyTorch to track `x`. After `y.backward()`, `x.grad`
holds dy/dx — for `y = x1^2 + x2^2` that's `2*x`, which is `[2., 2.]`.

## The sample

`autograd.py` does the same computation, then takes one manual gradient-descent
step inside `torch.no_grad()` (so the update itself isn't tracked). This is the
kernel of every training loop.

```bash
python3 autograd.py
```

## Try this

1. Change `y = (x * x).sum()` to `y = (x ** 3).sum()` and predict `x.grad`
   (it should be `3*x^2`).
2. Make `x = torch.randn(3, requires_grad=True)` and inspect the gradient.
3. Zero the gradient with `x.grad.zero_()` between backward passes and see why
   that matters.

## Going further

The 60-minute blitz is the canonical intro:
https://pytorch.org/tutorials/beginner/deep_learning_60min_blitz.html

spaces.tutorial:
# jQuery

jQuery is a browser library that wraps the DOM in one tidy function: `$`. You
select elements with CSS selectors (`$('.item')`), then chain methods to read
and change them, handle events, animate, and make AJAX calls. Modern browsers
have caught up on much of this, but jQuery is still everywhere and a great way
to learn DOM thinking.

## Get set up

jQuery runs *in the browser*, not in a terminal REPL. You need two things: a
way to serve the page, and the library itself.

- The sample loads jQuery from a **CDN** — that requires an internet
  connection. Offline? Download `jquery.min.js` from https://jquery.com/download/
  into this folder and change the `<script src>` to `"jquery.min.js"`.
- To serve the page we use Python's built-in web server (no install beyond
  Python itself).

## Run it

From this folder:

```bash
python3 -m http.server 8000
```

Then open **http://localhost:8000** in your browser. Click a list line — it
crosses off. Click **Add item** — a new line fades in.

> Opening `index.html` directly (file://) works too for this sample, but
> serving it is the habit that pays off once you add fetch/AJAX.

## The sample

`index.html` shows three core jQuery ideas:

- **Ready handler** — `$(function () { ... })` runs after the DOM is built.
- **Event delegation** — `$('#list').on('click', '.item', ...)` handles clicks
  on items that don't exist yet, which is why new items are clickable.
- **Chaining + animation** — `$('<li>').text(...).appendTo(...).hide().fadeIn()`
  builds, inserts, and animates in one expression.

## Try this

1. Add a **Clear done** button that runs `$('.done').remove()`.
2. Change `.fadeIn(200)` to `.slideDown(200)` and compare.
3. Log every click with `console.log($(this).text())`.

## Going further

The official learning center: https://learn.jquery.com/

spaces.tutorial:
# D3.js

D3 (Data-Driven Documents) is a JavaScript library for turning data into
visuals. Its signature move is the **data join**: you bind an array to a
selection of DOM/SVG elements, and D3 creates, updates, or removes elements to
match. Combined with **scales** (map data values to pixels) you can build any
chart you can imagine — D3 draws the SVG, you decide the design.

## Get set up

D3 runs in the browser. The sample loads it from a **CDN** (needs internet).
Offline? Grab `d3.v7.min.js` from https://d3js.org/ into this folder and point
the `<script src>` at it. To serve the page, use Python's built-in server.

## Run it

From this folder:

```bash
python3 -m http.server 8000
```

Open **http://localhost:8000**. You'll see a horizontal bar chart of four
quarters, each bar's length set by its value.

## The sample

`index.html` is a complete D3 bar chart in ~30 lines:

- **`d3.scaleLinear()`** maps a data value (0..max) to a pixel width so the
  chart auto-fits.
- **`selectAll('g').data(data).join('g')`** is the data join — one group per
  data point.
- Inside each group we `append` a `rect` (the bar) and two `text` labels, all
  driven by the datum `d`.

Change a number in the `data` array, reload, and the chart redraws.

## Try this

1. Add a fifth quarter to `data` — the join adds a bar with no other changes.
2. Color bars by value: `.attr('fill', d => d.value > 20 ? '#dc2626' : '#4f46e5')`.
3. Swap `scaleLinear` for `d3.scaleSqrt()` and see how the bars change.

## Going further

Work through the official tutorials and gallery: https://d3js.org/getting-started
