files.content:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8">
  <title>Three.js spinning cube</title>
  <style> body { margin: 0; overflow: hidden; } </style>
  <!-- Import map lets us `import 'three'` by name. Loads from a CDN,
       so this page needs an internet connection. -->
  <script type="importmap">
  {
    "imports": {
      "three": "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/three@0.160.0/build/three.module.js"
    }
  }
  </script>
</head>
<body>
  <script type="module">
    import * as THREE from 'three';

    // 1. Scene, camera, renderer — the three you always need
    const scene = new THREE.Scene();
    const camera = new THREE.PerspectiveCamera(75, innerWidth / innerHeight, 0.1, 100);
    camera.position.z = 3;

    const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({ antialias: true });
    renderer.setSize(innerWidth, innerHeight);
    document.body.appendChild(renderer.domElement);

    // 2. A mesh = geometry + material
    const cube = new THREE.Mesh(
      new THREE.BoxGeometry(),
      new THREE.MeshStandardMaterial({ color: 0x4f46e5 })
    );
    scene.add(cube);

    // 3. A light so the standard material is visible
    const light = new THREE.DirectionalLight(0xffffff, 3);
    light.position.set(2, 2, 3);
    scene.add(light);

    // 4. Animation loop
    renderer.setAnimationLoop(() => {
      cube.rotation.x += 0.01;
      cube.rotation.y += 0.013;
      renderer.render(scene, camera);
    });
  </script>
</body>
</html>

spaces.tutorial:
# Three.js

Three.js is the most popular way to do 3D in the browser. Raw WebGL is verbose;
Three.js gives you friendly building blocks — a **Scene** to hold objects, a
**Camera** to view them, **Meshes** (geometry + material) to draw, **Lights**
to illuminate, and a **Renderer** to paint it all to a canvas each frame.

## Get set up

Three.js runs in the browser and this sample uses an ES-module **import map**
that pulls Three.js from a **CDN** — so it needs an internet connection. Because
it uses `<script type="module">`, the page **must be served over http://**;
opening the file directly (file://) will fail with a CORS error. Use Python's
built-in server.

## Run it

From this folder:

```bash
python3 -m http.server 8000
```

Open **http://localhost:8000**. A purple cube spins in the middle of the
window. If you see a blank page, open the browser console — a missing internet
connection (CDN) or a `file://` URL are the usual culprits.

## The sample

`index.html` is a minimal but complete Three.js app, numbered in comments:

1. **Scene / Camera / Renderer** — the trio every app needs.
2. **Mesh** — a `BoxGeometry` wrapped in a `MeshStandardMaterial`.
3. **Light** — the standard material is dark without one.
4. **Animation loop** — `setAnimationLoop` nudges the cube's rotation and
   re-renders every frame.

## Try this

1. Swap `BoxGeometry` for `new THREE.TorusKnotGeometry()`.
2. Change the material color, or use `MeshBasicMaterial` (needs no light).
3. Add a second cube at `cube2.position.x = 1.5` and rotate it the other way.

## Going further

The official manual builds this exact scene step by step:
https://threejs.org/manual/#en/creating-a-scene

snippets: const reducer = (state = 0, action) => action.type === 'INC' ? state + 1 : state

install.mac: brew install node && npm install redux    # run npm install in this folder first
install.linux: sudo apt install nodejs npm && npm install redux    # run npm install in this folder first

spaces.tutorial:
# Redux

Redux is a predictable state container for JavaScript apps. The whole app state
lives in one **store**. You never mutate it directly — instead you `dispatch`
an **action** (a plain object like `{ type: 'INCREMENT' }`), and a **reducer**
(a pure function `(state, action) => nextState`) computes the new state. It's
pure JavaScript, so you can learn it right in Node, no React required.

## Get set up

You need Node.js, then install Redux **in this folder**:

```bash
npm install redux
```

(That creates `node_modules/` here so `require('redux')` resolves.)

## Your REPL

Start Node from this same folder and run the snippets in order (require first):

```bash
node
```

```js
> const { createStore } = require('redux')
> const reducer = (state = 0, action) => action.type === 'INC' ? state + 1 : state
> const store = createStore(reducer)
> store.dispatch({ type: 'INC' })
> store.getState()   // 1
```

Dispatch again and `getState()` climbs. That one-way flow — dispatch → reducer
→ new state → subscribers — is the entire idea.

## The sample

`counter.js` builds a counter with `INCREMENT`/`DECREMENT` actions and
**subscribes** a logger so you see state change on every dispatch:

```bash
node counter.js
```

Expect to see `1`, `2`, `1` printed as the three actions fire.

## Try this

1. Add a `'RESET'` action that returns `0`.
2. Change the initial state to `10` (the `state = 10` default in the reducer).
3. Make the reducer handle `{ type: 'ADD', amount: 5 }` using `action.amount`.

## Going further

The official tutorial (and why reducers must stay pure):
https://redux.js.org/tutorials/essentials/part-1-overview-concepts

spaces.name: RxJS
spaces.blurb: Reactive programming with Observables: compose async event streams like arrays.

install.mac: brew install node && npm install rxjs    # run npm install in this folder first
install.linux: sudo apt install nodejs npm && npm install rxjs    # run npm install in this folder first
install.windows: Install Node.js from nodejs.org, then in this folder: npm install rxjs

spaces.tutorial:
# RxJS

RxJS is reactive programming for JavaScript. Its core type is the
**Observable** — a stream of values over time (clicks, HTTP responses, timer
ticks). You compose streams with **operators** like `map`, `filter`, and
`take`, chained inside `.pipe(...)`, then `subscribe` to receive values. It
lets you treat async event streams with the same tools you'd use on an array.

## Get set up

Install Node.js, then RxJS **in this folder**:

```bash
npm install rxjs
```

## Your REPL

Start Node from this folder and run in order (require first):

```bash
node
```

```js
> const { of, interval } = require('rxjs')
> const { map, take, filter } = require('rxjs/operators')
> of(1, 2, 3).pipe(map(x => x * x)).subscribe(console.log)   // 1, 4, 9
> of(1, 2, 3, 4).pipe(filter(x => x % 2 === 0)).subscribe(console.log) // 2, 4
> interval(300).pipe(take(3)).subscribe(x => console.log('tick', x))
```

The last line is asynchronous — `interval` emits every 300ms and `take(3)`
completes the stream after three values, so it prints `tick 0/1/2` over ~1s.

## The sample

`streams.js` shows both flavors:

- A **synchronous** stream (`of(...)`) filtered to odds and scaled by 10.
- An **asynchronous** stream (`interval`) limited with `take(3)`, using the
  full observer form with a `complete` callback.

```bash
node streams.js
```

## Try this

1. Add `.pipe(map(x => x + 1))` after the filter and predict the output.
2. Replace `take(3)` with `take(5)` on the interval stream.
3. Import `{ from }` and try `from([10, 20, 30])` instead of `of`.

## Going further

The operator gallery and marble diagrams: https://rxjs.dev/guide/overview

spaces.tutorial:
# Tailwind CSS

Tailwind is a utility-first CSS framework. Instead of writing custom CSS, you
compose designs directly in your HTML from small, single-purpose classes
(`p-8`, `text-2xl`, `bg-white`, `flex`). A **build step** scans your files for
the classes you actually use and generates a tiny stylesheet with only those.
It's a *tool*, not a runtime library — you run its CLI.

## Get set up

You need Node.js. Install the CLI from the provided `package.json` **in this
folder**:

```bash
npm install
```

## Run it

Start the Tailwind build in watch mode. It reads `input.css` (which has the
`@tailwind` directives), scans `index.html` for class names, and writes
`output.css`:

```bash
npx tailwindcss -i input.css -o output.css --watch
```

Leave that running, then open **`index.html`** in your browser. You'll see a
styled card and button. Edit a class in `index.html` (say `bg-white` →
`bg-yellow-200`), save, and the watcher rebuilds `output.css` — reload to see
it.

> First run may create a `tailwind.config.js`; that's Tailwind telling the
> scanner which files to watch. The defaults work for this sample.

## The sample

- **`input.css`** — the three `@tailwind` directives plus a custom `.btn`
  component built with `@apply`.
- **`index.html`** — a card styled entirely with utility classes; it links the
  generated `output.css`.
- **`package.json`** — pins the `tailwindcss` CLI and adds `build`/`watch`
  scripts.

## Try this

1. Add `hover:scale-105 transition` to the card `div` and hover it.
2. Make the layout responsive: change padding to `p-4 md:p-8`.
3. Add a second button and give it `bg-emerald-600` via a new `@apply` rule.

## Going further

The utility reference is searchable and worth bookmarking:
https://tailwindcss.com/docs/utility-first

spaces.name: GraphQL
spaces.blurb: A typed query language for APIs: define a schema, ask for exactly the fields you want.

install.mac: brew install node && npm install graphql    # run npm install in this folder first
install.linux: sudo apt install nodejs npm && npm install graphql    # run npm install in this folder first
install.windows: Install Node.js from nodejs.org, then in this folder: npm install graphql

spaces.tutorial:
# GraphQL

GraphQL is a query language for APIs and a runtime that fulfills those queries.
The server publishes a **schema** — a typed graph of what data exists. The
client sends a **query** asking for exactly the fields it needs (no more, no
less), and the server runs **resolvers** to fill them in. One endpoint, no
over-fetching. This sample runs it all in one Node process so you can see the
request/response without any networking.

## Get set up

Install Node.js, then the reference implementation **in this folder**:

```bash
npm install graphql
```

## Run it

```bash
node server.js
```

You'll see printed JSON — the result of running the query against the schema:

```json
{
  "hello": "Hello, GraphQL!",
  "books": [ { "title": "Dune" }, { "title": "Neuromancer" } ]
}
```

Notice the query asked for `books { title }` — no `author` — so the response
omits authors. That precision is GraphQL's whole point.

## The sample

`server.js` has the three parts of every GraphQL setup, numbered in comments:

1. **Schema** (`buildSchema`) — declares `Book`, and a `Query` with `hello`
   and `books`.
2. **Resolvers** (`root`) — plain functions returning the data.
3. **Query + execution** — `graphql({ schema, source, rootValue })` runs it and
   returns `{ data }`.

## Try this

1. Add `author` to the query (`books { title author }`) and rerun.
2. Add a `count: Int` field to `Query` whose resolver returns `books().length`.
3. Give `books` an argument: `books(limit: Int)` and slice the array in the
   resolver.

## Going further

The official intro walks the same concepts: https://graphql.org/learn/

install.linux: sudo apt install nodejs npm && npm install && npx prisma generate    # install deps + generate the client

spaces.tutorial:
# Prisma

Prisma is a next-generation ORM for Node/TypeScript. You describe your data in
a declarative `schema.prisma` file, then Prisma **generates a fully typed
client** so queries like `prisma.user.findMany()` are autocompleted and
type-checked. It manages migrations and works with SQLite, Postgres, MySQL, and
more. This space uses SQLite so there's no database server to run.

## Get set up

Prisma needs Node.js and a couple of setup steps — be honest, there's more than
one command here. **In this folder:**

```bash
npm install            # install prisma + @prisma/client
npx prisma db push     # create dev.db and the tables from schema.prisma
npx prisma generate    # generate the typed client (required before seed.js)
```

## Run it

**Option A — browse the data in a GUI.** Prisma Studio opens a local web app:

```bash
npx prisma studio
```

It opens **http://localhost:5555** where you can view and edit rows. (Empty at
first — seed some data below.)

**Option B — seed and query from code:**

```bash
node seed.js
```

That creates a user with a nested post and prints everything back. Refresh
Prisma Studio and you'll see the rows.

## The sample

- **`schema.prisma`** — a SQLite datasource, the client generator, and two
  related models (`User` has many `Post`).
- **`seed.js`** — uses the generated client to `create` a user with a nested
  post, then `findMany` with a relation `include`.
- **`package.json`** — pins `prisma` and `@prisma/client` plus handy scripts.

## Try this

1. Add a `bio String?` field to `User`, then rerun `npx prisma db push`.
2. Query only published posts: `prisma.post.findMany({ where: { published: true } })`.
3. Update a row: `prisma.user.update({ where: { email: '...' }, data: { name: 'Al' } })`.

## Going further

The Quickstart uses this exact SQLite setup:
https://www.prisma.io/docs/getting-started/quickstart

install.linux: sudo apt install redis-server && sudo service redis-server start
install.windows: Use WSL (sudo apt install redis-server) or run Redis via Docker: docker run -p 6379:6379 redis

files.content:
# A tiny tour you can paste into redis-cli one line at a time.
# (Comments here are for you; redis-cli won't accept the # lines.)

# Strings
SET greeting hello
GET greeting

# Atomic counters
INCR pageviews
INCR pageviews
INCRBY pageviews 10

# Expiry: greeting self-destructs in 30 seconds
EXPIRE greeting 30
TTL greeting

# A list, used like a queue
RPUSH tasks email backup deploy
LRANGE tasks 0 -1
LPOP tasks

# A hash, like a small record
HSET user:1 name Alice age 30
HGETALL user:1

spaces.tutorial:
# Redis

Redis is an in-memory data store — a server that keeps data structures (strings,
counters, lists, hashes, sets) in RAM for microsecond access. It's the go-to
for caching, session storage, rate limiting, and queues. You interact with it
through `redis-cli`, a genuine REPL that speaks the Redis protocol to a running
server.

## Get set up

Redis is a **client and a server**. You must have a `redis-server` running
before the CLI can connect:

```bash
# macOS
brew install redis
brew services start redis      # runs the server in the background
```

Check it's up: `redis-cli PING` should answer `PONG`. If you get
`Could not connect`, the server isn't running.

## Your REPL

Start the CLI — the prompt shows the host and port:

```bash
redis-cli
```

```
127.0.0.1:6379> PING
PONG
127.0.0.1:6379> SET greeting hello
OK
127.0.0.1:6379> GET greeting
"hello"
127.0.0.1:6379> INCR counter
(integer) 1
127.0.0.1:6379> INCR counter
(integer) 2
```

`INCR` is atomic — run it repeatedly and the number climbs safely even under
concurrent clients. That's why Redis is a favorite for counters and rate limits.

## The sample

`session.redis` is a paste-along tour (feed it one non-comment line at a time)
covering the data types you'll use most: strings, atomic counters, key expiry
(`EXPIRE`/`TTL`), lists as queues (`RPUSH`/`LPOP`), and hashes as records
(`HSET`/`HGETALL`).

## Try this

1. `SET greeting hi EX 10` then watch `TTL greeting` count down.
2. Build a queue: `RPUSH jobs a b c`, then `LPOP jobs` three times.
3. `HINCRBY user:1 age 1` to bump a field inside a hash.

## Going further

An interactive tour of every command: https://redis.io/docs/latest/develop/

spaces.name: Docker Compose
spaces.blurb: Declare a multi-container app in one YAML file and bring it all up with one command.

install.mac: Install Docker Desktop from docker.com (includes the `docker compose` plugin)
install.linux: sudo apt install docker.io docker-compose-plugin && sudo systemctl start docker
install.windows: Install Docker Desktop from docker.com (includes the `docker compose` plugin)

files.content:
# A two-service app: Redis plus a tiny web page served by nginx.
# Bring it up:   docker compose up
# Tear it down:  docker compose down
services:
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"      # host:container — talk to it with redis-cli

  web:
    image: nginx:alpine
    ports:
      - "8080:80"        # open http://localhost:8080
    volumes:
      - ./site:/usr/share/nginx/html:ro
    depends_on:
      - redis            # start redis first

It's alive!

This page is served by the web (nginx) container.

A redis container is running alongside it on port 6379.

spaces.tutorial:
# Docker Compose

Docker Compose lets you define a whole multi-container application in one YAML
file — which images to run, their ports, volumes, and how they depend on each
other — then start everything with a single `docker compose up`. It's how you
run a database, a cache, and your app together on your laptop with one command.

## Get set up

Compose needs **Docker** installed and running. On macOS/Windows that's Docker
Desktop; on Linux install `docker.io` and the compose plugin. Confirm it works:

```bash
docker compose version
```

If that errors, Docker isn't installed or the daemon isn't running — start
Docker Desktop (or `sudo systemctl start docker`) first.

## Run it

From this folder:

```bash
docker compose up
```

The first run **pulls the images** (redis and nginx), which needs an internet
connection and can take a minute. Once it settles:

- Open **http://localhost:8080** — the nginx `web` service serves
  `site/index.html`.
- The `redis` service is live on port 6379 (try `redis-cli PING` if you have
  the CLI).

Stop it with **Ctrl-C**, then clean up the containers:

```bash
docker compose down
```

## The sample

`compose.yaml` declares two **services**:

- **`redis`** — the `redis:7-alpine` image, port 6379 published to your host.
- **`web`** — `nginx:alpine`, port 8080→80, mounting `./site` as its web root
  (read-only), and `depends_on: redis` so Redis starts first.

Change `site/index.html`, then reload the browser — the volume mount means no
rebuild needed.

## Try this

1. Add `environment: [ "NGINX_HOST=localhost" ]` under `web`.
2. Run it detached with `docker compose up -d`, then `docker compose logs -f`.
3. Add a third service (e.g. `postgres:16-alpine`) and give it a port.

## Going further

The Compose file reference and a step-by-step guide:
https://docs.docker.com/compose/gettingstarted/

spaces.name: Solidity
spaces.blurb: The language of Ethereum smart contracts — programs that hold real value, so every line counts.

install.windows: curl -L https://foundry.paradigm.xyz | bash && foundryup    # run in Git Bash; foundryup ships no native PowerShell installer

spaces.tutorial:
# Solidity

Solidity is the language of Ethereum smart contracts — small programs that live on a blockchain, hold real value, and cannot be patched after deploy. That changes how you write code: every type is explicit, every overflow reverts, and gas makes every operation cost something. The good news: the core language is small, and you can learn most of it at a REPL.

## Your REPL

Press **START** on the REPL device to launch `chisel`, Foundry's Solidity REPL. At the `➜` prompt every line is compiled and executed inside a scratch EVM. Click **HINTS** for starters, or type these:

```solidity
uint256 x = 42;
x * 2
keccak256(abi.encodePacked("hello"))
address(0)
type(uint8).max
```

`uint256` is the workhorse integer — 256 bits, no negatives. `keccak256` is Ethereum's hash; `abi.encodePacked` turns values into the bytes it eats. `address(0)` is the zero address, and `type(uint8).max` is 255 — arithmetic past it reverts rather than wrapping. Type `!help` for chisel's own commands (`!source` shows the contract your session is building).

## The sample

Open `Counter.sol` — the anatomy of every contract in one screen:

- **`pragma solidity ^0.8.24;`** pins the compiler range. 0.8+ gives you checked arithmetic for free.
- **`contract Counter { ... }`** is the unit of deployment — think class, but its fields live on-chain forever.
- **`uint256 public number;`** is a storage variable; `public` auto-generates a free getter called `number()`.
- **`event Increment(...)`** is the contract's logbook: cheap to emit, indexed off-chain, invisible to other contracts.
- **`setNumber` / `increment`** are transactions: anyone can call them, each call costs gas, and state persists between calls.

There's no `main` — a contract is deployed once, then its functions are entry points for the rest of its life.

## Try this

1. In chisel, paste the whole `Counter` contract, then run `Counter c = new Counter();` and `c.increment();` — call `c.number()` to see state stick.
2. Add a `decrement()` that refuses to go below zero: `require(number > 0, "at zero");` — then call it at zero and watch the revert.
3. Hash yourself an id: `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(address(0), uint256(7)))` — this pattern is how Solidity builds mapping keys.

## Going further

Install the toolchain (`curl -L https://foundry.paradigm.xyz | bash && foundryup`), then `forge init counter && cd counter && forge test` — the Foundry Book (https://book.getfoundry.sh) walks the whole road. The language reference lives at https://docs.soliditylang.org.

To quit the REPL, type `!quit` or press STOP.

spaces.name: Gleam
spaces.blurb: A friendly, type-safe language for the Erlang VM — Rust-flavored syntax, BEAM superpowers.

files.content:
name = "hello"
version = "1.0.0"

[dependencies]
gleam_stdlib = ">= 0.34.0 and < 2.0.0"

import gleam/io
import gleam/list
import gleam/int

pub fn main() {
  io.println("Hello, Gleam!")

  [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
  |> list.map(fn(n) { n * n })
  |> list.map(int.to_string)
  |> list.each(io.println)
}

spaces.tutorial:
# Gleam

Gleam is a statically typed language for the Erlang VM: Rust-flavored syntax, a friendly compiler that explains its errors, and the BEAM's legendary concurrency underneath. Everything compiles to plain Erlang, so the whole OTP ecosystem is yours.

## Your REPL

Gleam has no REPL of its own — click **START** and the studio runs `gleam shell`, which compiles this project and drops you into the *Erlang* shell with your Gleam modules loaded. Call them Erlang-style: `hello:main().` (note the trailing dot!). The stdlib compiles to modules like `gleam@list`.

## The sample

`src/hello.gleam` shows the pipe operator `|>` threading a list through map and print — the idiom Gleam shares with Elixir and F#. Run the whole program from the rack: aim at this folder and IGNITION's `gleam run` lane is already wired, as are FORGE (`gleam build`), VERITAS (`gleam test`), and CRATE (`gleam deps download`).

## Try

1. Add a function `pub fn double(x: Int) -> Int { x * 2 }` to `hello.gleam`, press START again, and call `hello:double(21).`
2. Break the types on purpose — change `x * 2` to `x <> "!"` — and read the compiler's error. Gleam's error messages teach the language.
3. `gleam add gleam_http` in SOLDER, then explore an HTTP client from the shell.

# Astro

Astro is the **content-first** framework: every file in `src/pages/` becomes a route, the frontmatter between `---` fences runs at build time, and the page ships as plain HTML — **zero JavaScript by default**. Interactivity is opt-in per component (“islands”).

## Your dev server

Press **START** and the rack runs `npm run dev` (astro dev). Open the URL that appears — or rack the **COSMOS** device, the Astro console, whose DEV button does the same with the URL wired to SCOPE and the version cluster watching the registry.

## The sample

`src/pages/index.astro` shows frontmatter feeding the template: `who` and `facts` are computed server-side and rendered to static HTML — view source in the browser and there is no framework runtime.

## Try

1. Add `src/pages/blog.astro` — it is instantly `/blog`, no router config.
2. Move a fact into the frontmatter as a computed value (`new Date().getFullYear()`); watch the dev server hot-reload.
3. `npm run build` then `preview` — the dist/ is pure static files, ready for any host.

---

## Install

If the tool isn't found when you press START, install it:

```sh
brew install node
```

spaces.tutorial:
# SvelteKit

SvelteKit is Svelte plus the app plumbing: a **filesystem router** (`src/routes/**/+page.svelte`), server rendering, and a build — all riding **Vite**, which is why `npm run dev` is `vite dev`.

## Your dev server

Press **START** for `vite dev` — or rack **KINETIC**, the SvelteKit console: DEV/BUILD/PREVIEW speak vite while DIAG runs svelte-check, and the version cluster tracks @sveltejs/kit.

## The sample

`+page.svelte` is a Svelte component: reactive `count` updates the DOM with no virtual DOM and no hooks — assignment IS reactivity.

## Try

1. Add `src/routes/blog/+page.svelte` — instantly `/blog`.
2. Add a `+page.js` beside a page exporting `load()` — its return value arrives as `data` in the page. That's the whole data story.
3. Break the types on purpose and run **DIAG** (svelte-check) on KINETIC.

---

## Install

```sh
brew install node
```

spaces.name: Nuxt
spaces.blurb: Vue with a filesystem router, server rendering, and auto-imports — pages/ becomes your routes.
spaces.tutorial:
# Nuxt

Nuxt is Vue plus the plumbing: a **filesystem router** (`pages/*.vue`), server rendering, and **auto-imports** — note the sample never imports `ref` or `NuxtLink`; Nuxt wires them.

## Your dev server

Press **START** for `nuxi dev` — or rack **NIMBUS**, the Nuxt console: DEV/BUILD/PREVIEW through nuxi, DIAG runs `nuxi typecheck`, and the version cluster tracks nuxt against the registry.

## The sample

`pages/index.vue` uses `<script setup>` with a reactive `ref` — Vue's composition API with none of the boilerplate.

## Try

1. Add `pages/blog.vue` — instantly `/blog`.
2. Add `server/api/hello.js` exporting `defineEventHandler(() => ({ hi: true }))` — that's a live API at `/api/hello`.
3. Run **DIAG** on NIMBUS to typecheck the whole app.

---

## Install

```sh
brew install node
```

spaces.name: Playwright
spaces.blurb: End-to-end tests that drive a real browser — and the SPECTER rack device speaks it natively.

install.mac: brew install node && npx playwright install
install.linux: sudo apt-get install -y nodejs npm && npx playwright install
install.windows: winget install OpenJS.NodeJS.LTS && npx playwright install

spaces.tutorial:
# Playwright

End-to-end tests drive a **real browser** — clicks, navigation, what the
user actually sees. Playwright is the modern standard: fast, parallel,
and able to record tests for you.

## 1. Install the pieces

You need Node (the rack told you if it's missing) and Playwright's
browsers, one time:

    npm install
    npx playwright install

## 2. First green run

Press **START** on the run device (or run `npx playwright test`). The two
starter tests visit a self-contained page — no server needed — click a
button, and assert what changed. You should see `2 passed`.

## 3. Meet SPECTER, the E2E console

Open the Task Rack (⌘9) and rack **SPECTER** from the VERIFY shelf —
this project's `playwright.config.ts` makes its ENGINE=auto resolve to
playwright:

- **RUN** runs the suite; flip **HEADED** to watch the browser work.
- **REPORT** serves the HTML report — it lands on the ⇄ serving chip
  and ⌘I like any dev server.
- **RECORD** opens the codegen recorder: click around any page and
  Playwright writes the test for you. If a dev server is running in the
  rack, RECORD aims at it automatically.
- **BROWSERS** re-runs `npx playwright install` when browsers are stale.

## 4. Test a real app

Set `baseURL` in `playwright.config.ts` to your dev server (VELOCITY's
URL, say), then `await page.goto("/")` — the **E2E Loop** preset wires
VELOCITY's READY jack straight into SPECTER's RUN so the suite fires
the moment the server is up.

## Troubleshooting

- `browserType.launch: Executable doesn't exist` → run
  `npx playwright install` (SPECTER's BROWSERS button does this).
- Tests pass locally but not in CI → export the rack patch
  (Project Studio ▸ Export CI) — SPECTER exports its RUN as a step.

spaces.name: Solid
spaces.blurb: You write JSX that compiles to fine-grained reactive updates - no virtual DOM, components run once.
spaces.tutorial:
# Solid

Solid looks like React but works differently: components run ONCE, and
only the reactive expressions re-run when signals change - no virtual
DOM diffing.

1. Run the dev driver (or rack VELOCITY: this is a Vite app - DEV
   serves it, the URL feeds SCOPE).
2. Open src/index.jsx. createSignal(0) returns a getter and a setter;
   note you CALL count() - that call is what subscribes the text node.
3. Change the button label; Vite hot-reloads instantly.
4. Try createEffect(() => console.log(count())) - it re-runs per click
   while the component function itself never re-runs.

The rack angle: VELOCITY for DEV/BUILD/PREVIEW with version currency,
and SPECTER runs a Playwright suite against the live URL.

spaces.name: Preact
spaces.blurb: A 3 kB React alternative with the same API - hooks and JSX included, compat alias optional.
spaces.tutorial:
# Preact

The same mental model as React in 3 kB: hooks, JSX, components - and
the preact/compat alias runs most React libraries unchanged.

1. Run the dev driver (this is a Vite app - VELOCITY drives it too).
2. src/index.jsx uses useState from preact/hooks - identical to
   React's. The difference is under the hood: real DOM diffing, no
   synthetic event system.
3. Add a second component and compose it; the bundle stays tiny -
   rack PRISM and weigh a production build against a budget.

spaces.name: Qwik
spaces.blurb: Resumability instead of hydration: state serializes and the client wakes only what you touch.
spaces.tutorial:
# Qwik

Qwik's trick is resumability: instead of re-running your components in
the browser (hydration), it serializes state and lazily wakes only the
event handlers you actually use - the $ suffixes mark the lazy-load
boundaries.

1. Run the dev driver (Vite underneath - VELOCITY speaks it).
2. src/main.tsx: component$ and onClick$ are code-split points; open
   the network tab and notice the handler chunk loads on first click,
   not on page load.
3. This space runs client-side (csr) for simplicity; Qwik City adds
   the server half.

spaces.tutorial:
# Lit

Lit is web components with the rough edges filed off: reactive
properties re-render automatically, and html tagged templates update
only the parts that changed.

1. Run the dev driver (plain Vite, no plugin needed - VELOCITY too).
2. src/count-button.js: static properties declares reactivity;
   assigning this.count schedules a re-render. Shadow DOM scopes the
   css styles.
3. Compare with Block Studio (Alt+Cmd+5): the components it composes
   are the same standard - customElements.define, shadow root, render
   on state change. Open one of its generated files here and read it
   with new eyes.

spaces.name: Alpine.js
spaces.blurb: Declarative behavior in your markup: x-data on a tag gives the page state and reactivity, no build step.

install.mac: brew install python3    # for the local web server; the library loads from a CDN in index.html

files.content: Hello from Alpine - no build step.

spaces.tutorial:
# Alpine.js

jQuery's spiritual successor: sprinkle x-data on any element and the
markup becomes reactive - no bundler, no components, no build step.

1. Run the driver (a plain static server; IGNITION serves this project
   the same way - it is a STATIC project).
2. index.html: x-data declares state, @click mutates it, x-text and
   x-show react. That is the whole model.
3. Add an x-init attribute that logs to the console to see the
   lifecycle; try an x-for template over a list.

The Classic Kit wizard (Tools menu) can vendor Alpine into any project
pinned + hashed, and the editor completes the Alpine.* JS API when the
page carries it.

spaces.tutorial:
# htmx

htmx extends HTML itself: any element can issue a request and swap the
response into the page - the server returns HTML, not JSON.

1. Run the driver (static server on :8000).
2. index.html: hx-get says where, hx-target says what to replace,
   hx-swap says how. Press the button and watch fragment.html land in
   the panel - check the network tab: a plain GET, HTML back.
3. Point hx-get at a real endpoint of your app (rack IGNITION or
   VELOCITY serving it) and you have server-driven UI with zero client
   code. The editor completes the htmx.* JS API when the page carries
   the script.
